File.Name: n101a_n101-005.jpg
Notes:
52601635603 Here is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) among cactus spines (and lichens!) in the garden this morning. I believe this is a juvenile of the species Araneus andrewsi spider with humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs. I have a prior shot of its web, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

After the driest start of any start of any year that I can recall, the month of December has been better. We got 4.25 inches yesterday and 17.8 inches of rain for the month -- and there's more on the way!

File.Name: n101b_n101-008.jpg
Notes: 52601532255 This is a group of beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms poking up through the fallen oak leaves in the woods today. This may actually be a related species of the mushroom because the cap isn't especially velvety or frosted as they sometimes are? The widely-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but brittle - like thin glass tubing. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

File.Name: n101c_n101-025.jpg
Notes: 52600599232 This is a brand new Earthstar "mushroom" just coming up in the soggy woods today. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy. It is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus). These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make a star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

File.Name: n102a_n102-009.jpg
Notes: 52603746859 I was looking under a piece of loose bark on a log in our woodpile today -- looking for slime molds or snails or whatever -- I found this tiny cricket! I believe it a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). This is small cricket, only about 3/16 inch long or less, not counting its antennae of course. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

File.Name: n102b_n102-013.jpg
Notes: 52603886855 This is a Woodlouse (Oniscidea, Isopoda, Crustacea) that cannot roll up into a ball. I call these "sow bugs" as distinct from the "pill bugs" that can roll into an into a ball. I found it under some bark in a woodpile. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

File.Name: n102c_n101-017.jpg
Notes: 52603676249 I'm pretty sure this is a transparent insect larva on a piece of oak bark in the soggy woods yesterday. Maybe it is the larva of a fly (Diptera)? It has an interesting "mouth". (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

File.Name: n103a_n103-001.jpg
Notes: 52605851044 I found this interesting spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. Maybe this spider is genus Kibramoa or Plectreurys in the spider family Plectreuridae? This one played dead when I found it, it even let me arrange its legs with a stick without complaining. I covered it up again with the bark after I got my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2023)

File.Name: n103b_n103-007.jpg
Notes: 52605589606 This is another Woodlouse (Oniscidea, Isopoda, Crustacea) that cannot roll up into a ball. It has remarkable eyes. I call these "sow bugs" as distinct from the "pill bugs" that can roll into an into a ball. I found it under some bark in a woodpile. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2023)

File.Name: n103c_n102-002.jpg
Notes: 52606076163 This is another tiny spider under a piece of loose bark in our woodpile yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

File.Name: n104a_n104-005.jpg
Notes: 52607561146 This is a big black beetle trying to get away from me after I exposed its hiding place under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile yesterday. It might be a Darkling Beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, but it's smaller and faster than the larger Pinacate Beetles (genus Coelocnemis or Eleodes) that I sometimes find. Maybe it is the species Live Oak Cluster Beetle (Cibdelis blaschkei)? (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2023)

File.Name: n104b_mc30-027.jpg
Notes: 52608049538 I'm pretty sure this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider web with raindrops. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2022)

File.Name: n104c_mc30-040.jpg
Notes: 52607964930 I'm pretty sure this is another Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider web with raindrops. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2022)

File.Name: n105a_n105-012.jpg
Notes: 52609991020 I found this handsome spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. I'm pretty sure this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

File.Name: n105b_n105-031.jpg
Notes: 52609972265 I found another big black beetle trying to get away from me after I exposed its hiding place under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile today, compare my photo from yesterday. It guess it is a Darkling Beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, maybe the species Live Oak Cluster Beetle (Cibdelis blaschkei). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

File.Name: n105c_n102-010.jpg
Notes: 52609795229 This is a tiny cricket showing its rear end. I believe it is a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). It was a under loose bark on a log in our woodpile a couple of days ago. This is small cricket, only about 3/16 inch long or less. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

File.Name: n106a_n106-010.jpg
Notes: 52612017835 I found these two different Ticks (Ixodidae, Arachnida) on dead stems of a perennial grass plant today -- probably Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae) though it hardly matters. They are male and female Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus). Males lacks a scutum or shield that allows the female to swell up during its blood meal. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't. These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us -- see this photo. Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2023)

File.Name: n106b_n105-026.jpg
Notes: 52611564951 Here are pleurotoid mushrooms growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) logs in our woodpile yesterday. Pleurotoid fungi all have a lateral stalk or no stalk at all, and take their name from the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus). That's probably what these are, but maybe not -- there are other kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

File.Name: n106c_n103-010.jpg
Notes: 52611977745 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) in the woods a couple of days ago. My first guess based on its color and the way it holds its wings would be a Root-Maggot Fly in the family Anthomyiidae. Any help with identification will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2023)

File.Name: n107a_n107-003.jpg
Notes: 52612987727 This is the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) in the flower scar on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. These crickets will become common -- and larger -- later in the spring, but they are just starting out. What's odd is that I've found them here before, see my photos here, here, here, here, and here. I don't know what the attraction is, but other crickets like it here as well -- see this photo of a different Tree Cricket on the fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; prickly pear cactus

File.Name: n107b_n107-009.jpg
Notes: 52613968713 I found these Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushrooms growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. Fresh caps sometimes have real blue or violet colors, see this photo, but they soon fade to tan or brown like this one with just a hint of violet. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)

File.Name: n107c_n107-015.jpg
Notes: 52613450301 This is Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin), a familiar Jelly Fungus in the Tremelliales in the woods today. It's nice to see after months of drought. They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. These odd "mushrooms" appear after rain and then dry out into a hard yellow crust as it looses moisture. Did you notice the tiny Globular Springtail (Symphypleona, Collembola)? (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)

File.Name: n108a_n107-020.jpg
Notes: 52616116064 Here are old and new brown-spored Agaricus mushrooms in deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. This mushroom has a sweet almond smell and tastes good in a nibble. I've been calling it the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens aka A. moronii). Another possibility is Agaricus silvicola. I suspect they are edible and delicious, but I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. "Once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)
associatedTaxa: Agaricus albolutescens; A. moronii; Agaricus silvicola

File.Name: n108b_n105-002.jpg
Notes: 52616107534 This is another group of beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms poking up through the fallen oak leaves in the woods today. This may actually be a related species of the mushroom because the cap isn't especially velvety or frosted as they sometimes are? The widely-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but brittle -- like thin glass tubing. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

File.Name: n108c_n105-005.jpg
Notes: 52616265490 Here is a photo of the little waterfall along our driveway -- it rarely flows, but here it is! It falls about 20 feet total. It's a small drainage and it takes some heavy rain to get it working. It's remarkable how quick it subsides when the rain slows down enough to get out with my camera. The woodland around the waterfall shows Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees, with an understory of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) and Wild Blackberry and still-dormant Poison Oak and Nuttall's Snowberry. I seem to get this shot whenever the waterfall is going, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; California Bay; Coastal Wood Fern; Wild Blackberry; Poison Oak; Nuttall's Snowberry
associatedTaxa: Dryopteris arguta; Umbellularia californica; Rubus ursinus; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Symphoricarpos mollis
Families: Fagaceae; Lauraceae; Dryopteridaceae; Rosaceae; Anacardiaceae; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: n109a_n109-010.jpg
Notes: 52618649408 Here is another photo of the little waterfall along our driveway -- it rarely flows, but it was cranking in the rain today! We've had over a foot of rain since yesterday when this storm began. More is coming. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2023)

File.Name: n109b_n102-034.jpg
Notes: 52617631867 I found this nice colony of pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods a couple of days ago. The gills start pallid, but in time they pick up the spore color like the upturned one. These don't have that chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool that some of these mushrooms have, like this one, so I guess they fall into the "Entoloma rhodopolium" group -- maybe Entoloma lividoalbum, but who knows. These are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees.

File.Name: n109c_n102-023.jpg
Notes: 52618402824 I believe these dark-spored mushrooms are part of the Psathyrella longipes group. They grow alone or in small groups on debris under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. The dark spore-covered gills don't turn to ink like the genus Coprinus, and they don't grow on dung like the genus Panaeolus. The caps are "hygrophanous" -- changing color as they dry out. They are attractive mushrooms albeit very fragile. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

File.Name: n110a_n110-005.jpg
Notes: 52620023281 I believe this is the mushroom formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus -- though I suspect the common name will stick. You can tell by the fallen leaves that oak trees are nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

File.Name: n110b_n110-023.jpg
Notes: 52620443270 Here is a bank of new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing with moss on a sandstone outcrop today. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive. They actually grow from a thick mass of rhizomes at the base of the fronds during our dry summer when they go dormant. These ferns are creatures of our shady woods in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

File.Name: n110c_n110-014.jpg
Notes: 52620437560 Here are tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a piece of bark from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. These look like little insect eggs, but something different will come from their spores, see this photo for a bit more info. I believe the photo shows sessile (without a stalk) sporangia or plasmodiocarps. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

File.Name: n111a_n111-025.jpg
Notes: 52622328290 Here are lots of tiny spiderlings on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm surprised they weren't washed away in the rain. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2023)

File.Name: n111b_n110-016.jpg
Notes: 52621887286 This is a spider and webbing and under a piece of loose bark in our woodpile. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

File.Name: n111c_n105-016.jpg
Notes: 52622134419 This is webbing under a piece of loose bark in our woodpile a couple of days ago -- or is it fungus mycelium? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

File.Name: n112a_n112-005.jpg
Notes: 52623741341 This is looking up into a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today, first new flower I've noticed. I like the heart-shaped anthers, though I would actually call them sagittate (arrowhead-like) rather than cordate (heart-like). If you look close, you can see little pinhead glands on the "petals" (that are really sepals, the real petals are white). Leaves, flower stalks, and the flowers themselves are all glandular in this species. See this photo for a comparison of our different native gooseberries. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

File.Name: n112b_n112-026.jpg
Notes: 52624212483 Here are two shots of a tiny orb-weaving spider taken today, but it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family instead of Araneidae. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides by the shape of its abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

File.Name: n112c_n110-013.jpg
Notes: 52623209527 This is the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) under loose bark on a log in our woodpile a couple of days ago. These crickets will become common -- and larger -- later in the spring, but they are just starting out. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

File.Name: n113a_n113-036.jpg
Notes: 52626044708 Here is a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on an Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaf in the woods today. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n113b_n113-031.jpg
Notes: 52625025852 These are Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae) that dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees in the woods today, see this photo. A small swarm of the midges was flying right by the spider's web with the expected results. It's curious that most (all?) of the flies are male with feathery antennae, see this photo for comparison shots of male and female. That makes me wonder if all the flies in the swarms are male. See this photo for another web with lots of these tiny flies. HFDF! File.Name: n113c_n113-029.jpg
Notes: 52626010963 Here is a Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. More properly, it is part of the Cortinarius collinitus group which has been split into several variants. Maybe they are really Cortinarius trivialis or Cortinarius glutinosoarmillatus, but the old name is familiar and works for me. They are very slimy mushrooms. You can also see remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stem below the cap, stained rusty-brown from falling spores -- see this photo for a closer look. That is typical for this genus. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Cortinarius trivialis; Cortinarius glutinosoarmillatus

File.Name: n114a_n113-004.jpg
Notes: 52627660469 This is a small colony of ants on the inside of my camera bag yesterday. They weren't here the day before. I don't understand the different types of ants, but there are differences: there's a winged ant that could be male and a larger one that could be queen. They have found shelter from the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)

File.Name: n114b_n112-030a.jpg
Notes: 52627877013 This cute little spider is a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the windshield on the little electric cart I use to get around in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

File.Name: n114c_n113-021.jpg
Notes: 52627866138 Here is another shot a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on an Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaf in the woods yesterday, see this photo. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n115a_n115-002.jpg
Notes: 52629681606 This is a tiny Barklouse (Psocoptera aka Psocodea) in our the rain gauge today! It's a more complicated rain gauge than the coffee can I used to use, as it has a small inner tube which measures hundredths of an inch and overflows into a larger outer tube which can hold up to ten inches. There's a funnel-like lid on top that directs water into the small tube. The critter seems to want to be inside and made no effort to get away when I took the lid off today. Compare this photo, this photo, and this photo of some other Barklice I've found. I don't notice these very often, though I suspect the woods are full of them. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

File.Name: n115b_n115-008.jpg
Notes: 52629665281 Here are the first new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods today. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar surprisingly intricate flowers, see this photo for a closer look at a single flower. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking -- but don't use too much, it's strong stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

File.Name: n115c_n115-042.jpg
Notes: 52629120392 Maybe these are Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. I sliced the gills with my knife, and they have latex that is white changing to yellow. They don't have the maple-syrup smell of the real Candy Cap (Lactarius fragilis). (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)
CommonNames: Rufous Candy Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n116a_n116-003.jpg
Notes: 52632312968 These are lovely little Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia) growing on sandstone in the woods. They are a fruticose lichen with a stalk, but they come from a foliose base. There are several different kinds, so I won't try to guess which one this is. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2023)

File.Name: n116b_n115-029.jpg
Notes: 52632252600 I found this Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods yesterday. Fresh caps sometimes have real blue or violet colors like this, but they soon fade to tan or brown with just a hint of violet, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

File.Name: n116c_n112-015.jpg
Notes: 52632054459 I found this Cortinarius mushroom pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees after our good rains a few days ago. Like all Cortinarius mushrooms, this one has rusty-colored spores which stain everything - especially the shaggy hairs on its stem that originally form a veil to protect the gills. The problem is they end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart -- but they are handsome mushrooms when they are fresh like this. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

File.Name: n117a_n117-004.jpg
Notes: 52634302455 This is another small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2023)

File.Name: n117b_n117-011.jpg
Notes: 52633844761 I found another Cortinarius mushroom pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees after our recent good rains today. Like all Cortinarius mushrooms, this one has rusty-colored spores. The problem is they end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart -- but they are handsome mushrooms when they are fresh like this. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2023)

File.Name: n117c_n113-024.jpg
Notes: 52634275585 These are the tunnels of leaf-miner caterpillars meandering through a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. You can follow the path of the caterpillars' life from a small start to a big finish, growing as they move along. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)

File.Name: n118a_n115-005.jpg
Notes: 52636043274 Here is a mushroom-like spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These are "bisexual galls" because the will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual "oak apple" galls, see this photo, which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next year and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that. It's part of what makes then so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2023)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n118b_n115-032.jpg
Notes: 52636252883 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is a caterpillar and webbing (and raindrops) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

File.Name: n118c_n115-014.jpg
Notes: 52636201210 This is a webbing under a piece of loose bark in our woodpile. I have another photo of the spider in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

File.Name: n119a_n119-012.jpg
Notes: 52637064627 I believe this is a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hiding under a leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2023)

File.Name: n119b_n119-005.jpg
Notes: 52637056577 I found this small spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. I'm pretty sure this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, maybe the same kind as this one. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2023)

File.Name: n119c_n115-020.jpg
Notes: 52637805179 These are different critters under a piece of oak bark in our woodpile. I believe there is a Jumping Bristletail (Machilidae) on the left and a Woodlouse (Isopoda) on the right. The woodlouse is showing its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

File.Name: n120a_n120-013.jpg
Notes: 52639203446 This is a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on old stalk of a Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2023)

File.Name: n120b_n120-019.jpg
Notes: 52639667593 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a old leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) in the woods today. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2023)

File.Name: n120c_n118-030.jpg
Notes: 52639660118 This is a Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae) that usually dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees in the woods a couple of days ago, compare this photo. This one is male with feathery antennae, see this photo for comparison shots of male and female. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2023)

File.Name: n121a_n121-016.jpg
Notes: 52640539687 I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) looking at its shadow on a fallen oak leaf today. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

File.Name: n121b_n121-006.jpg
Notes: 52641294199 This is another shot of a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today, see this photo. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

File.Name: n121c_n120-003.jpg
Notes: 52641027931 I found this small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on an old leaf of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods yesterday. Its heavily spined legs give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2023)

File.Name: n122a_n122-012.jpg
Notes: 52643687980 Here are tiny spiders on stems of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods today. I believe they are Mesh Web Weaver spiders in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself -- at least it matches the illustration for that genus in my book. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2023)

File.Name: n122b_n121-018.jpg
Notes: 52642702447 I found these mysterious orbs in leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. Maybe they are eggs or pupal cases? They look like are wrapped in silk. It reminds me of craft projects I've seen with string or paper mache wrapped around a balloon with glue, then the balloon is popped after the glue dries - like this. I wonder how it was made without a balloon? Is there something in here?? I've found similar ones in the woods, see this photo and this photo. I love finding these little mysteries. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

File.Name: n122c_n121-023.jpg
Notes: 52643180811 This is the tunnel of a tiny leaf-miner on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods yesterday. I suppose it's another leaf-miner caterpillar (or several?), but compare this one and this one -- it appears to have many branches. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

File.Name: n123a_n123-021.jpg
Notes: 52645820245 This is of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae in the canyon today. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

File.Name: n123b_n123-003.jpg
Notes: 52645622324 This is another small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in the woods today, but this one is on a different plant -- native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed, see this photo. I believe this is another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

File.Name: n123c_n123-005.jpg
Notes: 52645796545 Here are new flowers of (I believe) our native dry-land Round-fruited Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in our canyon today. You can see the shaggy male staminate flowers on the left, and the female pistillate flowers are already developing into seeds lower down on the stems. It's a remarkably tough grass-like plant that grows in the shaded woods, but also in glaring sun. It's not at all like a typical wet-land sedge, except for the flowers. I really admire the toughness of this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

File.Name: n124a_n124-003.jpg
Notes: 52647407831 Here is another Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly among fallen oak leaves in the woods today. This is one of a group of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days -- including the Mourning Cloak and California Tortoiseshell. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2023)

File.Name: n124b_n124-024.jpg
Notes: 52647870343 I found this mysterious orb in a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, see this photo. Now I believe it is the pupa of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect, see this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2023)

File.Name: n124c_n124-006.jpg
Notes: 52647857498 This is a liverwort (Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon, but I believe it is different from the usual California Liverwort (Asterella californica). For one thing, it's growing in a sunnier place. The sporophytes are taller and smoother and less divided, and the creeping "leaves" ("thallus") are smaller, compare this photo. This one might be Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri), see the photos at CalPhotos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2023)

File.Name: n125a_n125-004.jpg
Notes: 52649750943 Here is a Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) on the chair that I use in the woods. Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2023)

File.Name: n125b_n125-011.jpg
Notes: 52649259746 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is webbing on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and the frass (poop) shows that it's the work of a caterpillar rather than a spider. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2023)

File.Name: n125c_n122-006.jpg
Notes: 52649730683 This is a spider web on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed a couple of days ago. I suspect this is the web of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, it's near the spider in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2023)

File.Name: n126a_n126-008.jpg
Notes: 52651603793 I believe this is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a mossy rock in the woods today. It is uncommonly handsome! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

File.Name: n126b_n123-010.jpg
Notes: 52651550945 I found this tiny Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on an old leaf of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods a few days ago. Its heavily spined legs give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

File.Name: n126c_n120-009.jpg
Notes: 52650605042 This is another shot of a tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on old stalk of a Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods a few days ago, compare this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2023)

File.Name: n127a_n127-015.jpg
Notes: 52653159646 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a sandstone outcrop in in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2023)

File.Name: n127b_n126-013.jpg
Notes: 52653149586 This is a another Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a fallen oak leaf yesterday. This fly is the Eristalini tribe, possibly the genus Eristalis or Asemosyrphus polygrammus aka Lejops polygrammus -- compare this photo and this photo taken a few years ago. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2023)

File.Name: n127c_n124-011.jpg
Notes: 52653414754 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods a few days ago. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2023)

File.Name: n128a_n128-003.jpg
Notes: 52655336169 I found this skeletonized leaf on a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This may be the work of a caterpillar like the one in this photo, but fungi also do this. I'm not sure how to tell the actions apart when a leaf gets to this stage, but they are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2023)

File.Name: n128b_n127-019.jpg
Notes: 52655491415 I believe this is a male Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hiding under a leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2023)

File.Name: n128c_n127-008.jpg
Notes: 52655487605 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods yesterday. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. This time I was ready! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2023)

File.Name: n129a_n129-009.jpg
Notes: 52657754748 I found this interesting spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. Maybe this spider is genus Kibramoa in the spider family Plectreuridae -- and maybe Kibramoa madrona)? This one played dead when I found it, and I covered it up again with the bark after I got my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2023)

File.Name: n129b_n129-020.jpg
Notes: 52657249901 Here are new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods today. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar surprisingly intricate flowers, see this photo for a closer look at a single flower. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking -- but don't use too much, it's strong stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2023)

File.Name: n129c_n129-004.jpg
Notes: 52657736673 Here is a bank of new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing with moss on a sandstone outcrop today. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive. They actually grow from a thick mass of rhizomes at the base of the fronds during our dry summer when they go dormant. These ferns are creatures of our shady woods in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2023)

File.Name: n130a_n130-007.jpg
Notes: 52659180992 This is a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. I got the photo in light rain today that was just right to leave a few raindrops. If you look close, you can see little pinhead glands on the "petals" (that are really sepals, the real petals are white). Leaves, flower stalks, and the flowers themselves are all glandular in this species. See this photo for a comparison of our different native gooseberries. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2023)

File.Name: n130b_n130-009.jpg
Notes: 52660079710 This is a new plant of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This will become the violet form of these plants, see this photo for the lavender flowers. See this photo and this photo of other forms. These plants seem to be true biennials which form leaves in their first year, then flower in their second year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2023)

File.Name: n130c_n130-021.jpg
Notes: 52659132362 This is a bank of liverworts (Marchantiophyta) growing on sandstone in the woods today. I believe these are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). These liverworts fade to black as they dry out and recover in a day with some rain, compare this photo. In time they will develop little sexual sporophytes, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2023)

File.Name: n131a_n131-027.jpg
Notes: 52661208182 Here is a striking fly (Diptera) in the woods today that looks for all the world like a bee. I believe this is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), possibly a Narcissus Bulb Fly (Merodon equestris) or kin in the same Eristalini tribe that includes many more bee-mimic flies in the genus Eristalis. It's furry like a bee and even has flattened leg segments like a bee. It's my understanding that this fly has been introduced from Europe and is now widespread in North America. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2023)

File.Name: n131b_n131-020.jpg
Notes: 52661695971 These are new leaves of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in woods today. These shrubs are drought-deciduous, and the plants looked quite dead a month ago. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2023)

File.Name: n131c_n131-014.jpg
Notes: 52662122640 Here are many galled flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). That odd beaked shape is typical. I'm pretty sure these are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2023)

File.Name: n201a_n201-004.jpg
Notes: 52664073344 Here is tiny spider on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods today. I believe this is a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself -- at least it matches the illustration for that genus in my book. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2023)

File.Name: n201b_n201-027.jpg
Notes: 52664063994 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods today. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2023)

File.Name: n201c_n130-030.jpg
Notes: 52663257907 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods a couple of days ago -- with raindrops. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see, see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2023)

File.Name: n202a_n202-011.jpg
Notes:
52665244637 This is a tiny spider on a cactus spine in the garden this morning. It is a well-endowed male, maybe a Dwarf Spider (Erigoninae) in the Linyphiidae? (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2023)

Today is Groundhog Day, midway between solstice and equinox, and we are getting more light in our winter-shady canyon. any groundhog or gopher that stuck its head out today did not see its shadow - portending early spring?

File.Name: n202b_n202-004.jpg
Notes: 52666234943 This is the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) in the flower scar on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. These crickets will become common -- and larger -- later in the spring. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2023)

File.Name: n202c_n202-028.jpg
Notes: 52666016569 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2023)

File.Name: n203a_n203-006.jpg
Notes: 52667424056 I believe this is a Stonefly in the order Plecoptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) near the creek in the woods today. I've never seen one in our canyon before, but the creek is running and they like running water. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2023)

File.Name: n203b_n202-027.jpg
Notes: 52667712144 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2023)

File.Name: n203c_n131-028.jpg
Notes: 52667921373 Here is a striking fly (Diptera) in the woods today that looks for all the world like a bee. I believe this is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), possibly a Narcissus Bulb Fly (Merodon equestris) or kin in the same Eristalini tribe that includes many more bee-mimic flies in the genus Eristalis. It's furry like a bee and even has flattened leg segments like a bee. It's my understanding that this fly has been introduced from Europe and is now widespread in North America. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2023)

File.Name: n204a_n204-017.jpg
Notes: 52669751383 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2023)

File.Name: n204b_n204-010.jpg
Notes: 52669521879 This is two shots of a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, see this photo and this photo. It seems to moving into a new home. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2023)

File.Name: n204c_n204-005.jpg
Notes: 52668726402 Here are lots of new galls on leaves of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe they are the galls of the Sage Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia) fly in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2023)

File.Name: n205a_n205-026.jpg
Notes: 52671772689 This is an odd Cup Fungus (Pezizaceae or kin) growing in mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. It appears to be different from the previous photo. Did you notice the tiny Springtail (Collembola) insects on the top-tight? (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2023)
CommonNames: Cup Fungus; Coast Live Oak; Springtail

File.Name: n205b_n205-019.jpg
Notes: 52671747839 Maybe these odd mushrooms are the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin), but I really don't know. I found a number of these in deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. There is no stalk to speak of and no ribs at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2023)
CommonNames: Spreading Brown Cup Fungus; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n205c_n205-020.jpg
Notes: 52671438096 This is a brand new Earthstar "mushroom" just coming up in the woods today. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy. It is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus). These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make a star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2023)

File.Name: n206a_n206-015.jpg
Notes: 52674230788 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a sandy place in the canyon today. This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders that I've been showing lately, but something else. It resembles a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely genus Ozyptila with stubby front legs with just two spines on the first tibia. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2023)

File.Name: n206b_n206-025.jpg
Notes: 52674209313 Here is a pair of tiny Ticks (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) waiting at the end of a old stem in the woods yesterday. This is a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus). They are the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently our lizards protect us -- see this photo for the fantastic story. This tick is small, only about 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2023)

File.Name: n206c_n202-026.jpg
Notes: 52673699341 I believe this is a Western Boxelder Bug (Boisea rubrolineata) in the family Rhopalidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) a few days ago. It's interesting that the closest Boxelder (Acer negundo, Aceraceae) tree is about 20 miles away down the river past Buellton, but we do have Bigleaf Maple (Acer grandifolium) not far away in the canyon, and I guess that's close enough. I've found the same bugs in Oregon, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2023)

File.Name: n207a_n207-002.jpg
Notes: 52675738021 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods today. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. This time I was ready! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2023)

File.Name: n207b_n207-011.jpg
Notes: 52675212632 This is a damaged leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2023)

File.Name: n207c_n206-013.jpg
Notes: 52676141200 Here is another shot of the tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a sandy place in the canyon yesterday. This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders that I've been showing lately, but something else. It may be a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus or a genus Ozyptila. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2023)

File.Name: n208a_n208-013.jpg
Notes: 52677592721 Here are two shots of the same little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers on its web today. You can see it in its little house on the left, but it came out of its house and posed for me on the right! These little orb weavers make a cobwebby web for their house, with a proper orb web out front. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2023)

File.Name: n208b_n208-029.jpg
Notes: 52678084068 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods today. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2023)

File.Name: n208c_n205-010.jpg
Notes: 52677863869 This is a spider web after a small rain. The plant is native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum, Polemoniaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2023)

File.Name: n209a_n209-013.jpg
Notes: 52679737319 This is a tiny Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) on its web in the woods today. This spider is small, about 3/16 inch. It's anchored by one end of its line and holding the other end, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2023)

File.Name: n209b_n208-018.jpg
Notes: 52679726279 This is another shot of the tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) that I found on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2023)

File.Name: n209c_n208-007.jpg
Notes: 52679867560 Here is another shot of the same little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) that I showed yesterday. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2023)

File.Name: n210a_n210-003.jpg
Notes: 52681755070 I found this odd capsule on a dead leaf of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's about the size of a grain of rice. I believe this is the pupal case of an caterpillar, see the shot on the right which the shows the remains of it. I've found something similar before, see this photo and this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2023)

File.Name: n210b_n210-023.jpg
Notes: 52680800547 This is another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging in the inner circle of her web. These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2023)

File.Name: n210c_n204-002.jpg
Notes:
52681294906 These are the light-trails of tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams with a background of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae). I guess they are tiny Midges in the family Chironomidae, see this photo and this photo. I assume this is a mating thing. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2023)

These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so -- close, but not too close. Then the trick to getting these photos is to slow your camera down. I got this photo at 0.2 sec @ f/11 @ ISO 100, on a tripod of course. Some post-processing to bump up the contrast helps. In some years, we have large mating swarms of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees -- see this photo from four years ago. You'd think the flies would be an easy target for birds and other predators, but it's like swimming into a school of anchovies -- they're all around but impossible to touch.

File.Name: n211a_n211-020.jpg
Notes: 52683615308 This is a empty acorn cap with a tiny spider inside! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2023)

File.Name: n211b_n211-014.jpg
Notes: 52683388954 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These wasps have no sign of a "wasp-waist" unlike most of their cousins. They are just starting to appear this year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2023)

File.Name: n211c_n208-022.jpg
Notes: 52683523235 Here is a small Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) in in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods a couple of days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2023)

Notes: 52685505926 I believe this is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hiding under a leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2023)

File.Name: n212c_n212-026.jpg
Notes: 52685729749 These are pistillate catkins of a Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) tree down by the creek today. File.Name: n213b_n213-002.jpg

(San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2023)

File.Name: n215a_n212-022.jpg
Notes: 52691984013 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods a few days ago -- with the spider. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2023)

File.Name: n215b_n211-002.jpg
Notes: 52691905560 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods a few days ago -- with raindrops. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2023)

File.Name: n215c_n211-018.jpg
Notes: 52691748404 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is webbing on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and the frass (poop) shows that it's the work of a caterpillar rather than a spider. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2023)

File.Name: n301a_n228-008.jpg
Notes:
52719801012 Here is a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) that I believe is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae -- their shape is pretty distinctive. I found it on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) in the woods yesterday, but there is Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) about ten feet away. The wasp is tiny, about 1/8 inch. I have hundreds of photos of different wasp galls but few of the wasps themselves, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2023)

I haven't been able to get outside for a couple of weeks because I hurt my knee in a fall. It feels good to be back.
CommonNames: Gall Wasp; Hummingbird Sage; Scrub Oak

File.Name: n301b_n228-006.jpg
Notes: 52720792578 Here is a Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides, Pholcidae) in the bathroom of our house -- with another spider for its prey. At least it's some kind of Cellar Spider in that family. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2023)

File.Name: n301c_n226-022.jpg
Notes: 52720780628 These are new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica, Brassicaceae) with snow with our storm in the woods a few days ago . I found this native plant growing above our retaining wall at home, and it's still the first and only herbaceous plant I've seen with flowers this year in the canyon at home. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but please don't dig it up -- it tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2023)

File.Name: n302a_n302-022.jpg
Notes: 52722736655 Here is a tiny orb-weaving spider taken today, but it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family instead of Araneidae. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides by the shape of its abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2023)

File.Name: n302b_n302-005.jpg
Notes: 52721779702 This is a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, see this photo. I don't know how this critter survived the snow last weekend. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2023)

File.Name: n302c_n228-020.jpg
Notes: 52721767027 Here is another photo on the tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) that I believe is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae, see this photo from yesterday. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2023)

File.Name: n303a_n303-002.jpg
Notes: 52724750313 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2023)

File.Name: n303b_n303-024.jpg
Notes: 52724733663 This is a mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on pistillate catkin of a Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek today. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the catkins. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2023)

File.Name: n303c_n302-016.jpg
Notes: 52723713182 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it has interesting colors. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2023)

File.Name: n304a_n304-022.jpg
Notes: 52730456502 This is a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2023)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n304b_n304-010.jpg
Notes: 52730449467 This is tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider hiding in a leaf ofCoast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2023)

File.Name: n304c_n304-014.jpg
Notes: 52731376650 Here are new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods today. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar surprisingly intricate flowers, see this photo for a closer look at a single flower. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking -- but don't use too much, it's strong stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2023)

File.Name: n305a_n305-001.jpg
Notes: 52732511897 Here are lots of new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2023)

File.Name: n305b_n305-022.jpg
Notes: 52732500302 This is front and back of a critter in an oak leaf, probably a leaf-miner caterpillar. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2023)

File.Name: n305c_n305-010.jpg
Notes: 52733018451 I found this skeletonized leaf on a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. This may be the work of a caterpillar like the one in this photo, but fungi also do this. I'm not sure how to tell the actions apart when a leaf gets to this stage, but they are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2023)

File.Name: n306a_n306-009.jpg
Notes:
52731416365 Here is a tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) in a new flower of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is a Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae, see this photo for a whole lot of them a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2023)

Our landline phone was out this weekend and our internet connection as well. Bah, this is no way to run a business -- or a public utility.

File.Name: n306b_n306-016.jpg
Notes: 52731482553 This is another tunnel of a leaf-miner caterpillar meandering through a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can follow the path of the caterpillar's life from a small start to a big finish, growing as it moves along. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2023)

File.Name: n306c_n306-004.jpg
Notes: 52730989246 Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know! These are new leaves of a perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon still holding raindrops from last night. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2023)

File.Name: n307a_n307-013.jpg
Notes: 52733313904 This is a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods today. Maybe it is a juvenile of the species Araneus andrewsi spider with humps on its back and bristly legs? (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2023)

File.Name: n307b_n307-026.jpg
Notes: 52733540118 This is looking at a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods today. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. This time I was ready! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2023)

File.Name: n307c_n307-016.jpg
Notes: 52733456590 Here are lots of flowers of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) growing in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2023)

File.Name: n308a_n308-049.jpg
Notes: 52734645487 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today -- with prey, a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera). I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2023)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak; Common Sawfly

File.Name: n308b_n308-019.jpg
Notes: 52735162366 This is another empty acorn cap with a tiny spider inside! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2023)

File.Name: n308c_n303-018.jpg
Notes: 52735631023 This is looking at a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods a few days ago. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, compare this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2023)

File.Name: n309a_n309-011.jpg
Notes: 52737792443 This is a Sara Orangetip aka Pacific Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly on native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These are lovely butterflies that deserve a better photo (like this), but they've been flying up and down the canyon and rarely stop to feed. Even when they do stop, they are quite camera shy, at least this early in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2023)
CommonNames: Sara Orangetip; Pacific Orangetip; Golden Yarrow

File.Name: n309b_n309-019.jpg
Notes: 52737299121 This is a rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2023)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n309c_n309-021.jpg
Notes: 52737688345 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. I got the photo the right today and the photo the right yesterday, see this photo. Its prey is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera). I believe this spider is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March and 8 March 2023)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak; Common Sawfly

File.Name: n310a_n308-040.jpg
Notes: 52739685273 I found this atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a oak leaf in the woods a couple of days ago. I've seen these little flies before, see this photo and this photo. I believe this fly is in tribe Phthiriini, see this photo at BugGuide. Nice eyes and great whiskers -- HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2023)

File.Name: n310b_n308-001.jpg
Notes: 52739188861 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on the flower scar on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus a couple of days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2023)

File.Name: n310c_n308-023.jpg
Notes: 52739594670 Here is a tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) in a empty acorn cap in the woods a couple of days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2023)

File.Name: n311a_n309-010.jpg
Notes: 52741504129 These are the first new flowers and seed pods of Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The flowers are quite tiny, only a couple of mm across. The seed pods become long cylinders. I believe this tiny mustard is native, but it grows like a weed in our mountains. It's easy to walk right past it without noticing. I think the Sara Orangetip butterfly in this this photo was interested. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2023)

File.Name: n311b_n308-010.jpg
Notes: 52741714843 This is a neat little forest of sexual sporophytes of (I guess) the California Liverwort (Asterella californica, Marchantiophyta). They remind me of little surfing palm trees! They seem to be having a good year with all the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2023)

File.Name: n311c_n308-025.jpg
Notes: 52740695882 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family. I really think that this plant's outstanding feature is the foliage, though the flowers and seeds are attractive. I wonder if this plant is the original host plant for Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies before they adopted non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2023)

File.Name: n312a_n312-028.jpg
Notes: 52743892099 This is an big-eyed male March Fly (family Bibionidae, Diptera) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe this is the genus Bibio, probably Bibio albipennis with those long white hairs. ("Albipennis" is Latin for "white feathers", and those hairs are kind of feather-like.) It's eyes are remarkable -- and hairy. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2023)

File.Name: n312b_n312-010.jpg
Notes: 52743879304 Here are ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) burrowing into in a new flower of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2023)

File.Name: n312c_n312-022.jpg
Notes: 52743084602 Here is a little patch of moss (Bryophyta) -- with sporophytes -- growing on a rock outcrop in the woods. A little rain changes everything. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2023)

File.Name: n313a_n313-032.jpg
Notes: 52746502538 I found this a large Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae) on the chair on the deck today. It's named for the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. It might well be the Western Leaf-footed Bug (Leptoglossus clypealis) with a spine extending forwards from the tip of the head. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

File.Name: n313b_n313-018.jpg
Notes: 52746247244 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaf in the woods today. I believe this spider is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

File.Name: n313c_n313-001.jpg
Notes: 52746238169 This is a little caterpillar on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

File.Name: n314a_n313-012.jpg
Notes: 52747569977 This is another shot of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on its web in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

File.Name: n314b_n313-007.jpg
Notes: 52748573938 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

File.Name: n314c_n313-028.jpg
Notes: 52747530157 This is a another shot of the large Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae) on the chair on the deck yesterday, see this photo. It's named for the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. It might well be the Western Leaf-footed Bug (Leptoglossus clypealis) with a spine extending forwards from the tip of the head. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

File.Name: n315a_n315-010.jpg
Notes: 52750558853 Here is the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) with raindrops from our storm last night. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2023)

Notes: 52750464445 This is a web of a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) spider under a leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2023)

File.Name: n315c_n311-027.jpg
Notes: 52750455520 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods a few days ago. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2023)

Notes: 52752509285 This is a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) that is interested in a Stink Bug (Pentatomidae) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2023)
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Stink Bug; Coast Live Oak

Notes: 52752499950 Here is the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

Notes: 52754347459 Here is a Chaparral Camel Cricket balanced on a leaf of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

Notes: 52754343674 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

Notes: 52754092376 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago. I don't know what kind of fly it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2023)

File.Name: n318a_n318-012.jpg
Notes: 52756063516 This is another tunnel of a leaf-miner caterpillar meandering through a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can follow the path of the caterpillar's life from a small start to a big finish, growing as it moves along. Is that the real caterpillar in the end of the tunnel?? (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2023)

File.Name: n318b_n318-011.jpg
Notes: 52756460645 These are tiny liverworts (Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon, but I believe it is different from the usual California Liverwort (Asterella californica). For one thing, it's growing in a sunnier place. The sporophytes are taller and smoother and less divided, and the creeping "leaves" ("thallus") are smaller, compare this photo. This one might be Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri), see the photos at CalPhotos. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2023)

File.Name: n318c_n317-004.jpg
Notes: 52756293179 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaf in the woods yesterday. I believe this spider is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2023)

File.Name: n319a_n318-005.jpg
Notes: 52758842483 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. I don't know what kind of fly it is. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2023)

File.Name: n319b_n316-021.jpg
Notes: 52758594629 Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know! This a new leaf of a perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon still holding raindrops from the night before. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2023)

File.Name: n319c_n312-042.jpg
Notes: 52758739630 This is another shot of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on its web in the woods with raindrops. Did you notice the tiny Springtail (Collembola) insect before it? (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2023)

File.Name: n320a_n320-031.jpg
Notes:
52760215997 This is a Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) on shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It's the first of these proper Crane Flies with long palps that I've seen this year. I don't know if it's the true Giant Western Crane Fly (Holorusia hespera), but it seems big enough -- probably a couple of inches across. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2023)

Today is the vernal equinox and the first official day of spring. Happy equinox! The seasons change, life goes on.
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Giant Western Crane Fly

File.Name: n320b_n320-022.jpg
Notes: 52761143960 This is a little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. There are many different kinds of these weevils, but maybe this one is species Thricolepis inornata, see this photo. This is a the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2023)

File.Name: n320c_n320-006.jpg
Notes: 52760727466 As I've said before, Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know. These are new leaves of a perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon still holding raindrops from the last night. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2023)

File.Name: n321a_n321-002.jpg
Notes: 52763265959 Look close! This is a well-camouflaged caterpillar on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's doing some damage. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2023)

File.Name: n321b_n320-028.jpg
Notes: 52763407230 Here is a small Beetle (Coleoptera) on a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods yesterday. Maybe this is in the Leaf Beetle (Chrysomelidae) family? (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2023)

File.Name: n321c_n320-001.jpg
Notes: 52763474958 These are new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica, Brassicaceae) with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2023)

File.Name: n322a_n318-020.jpg
Notes: 52765506998 This is Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider web in small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods a few days ago. You can see the tiny spider on its retreat to the side. It is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2023)

File.Name: n322b_n318-007.jpg
Notes: 52765420515 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods a few days ago. The spider's orb web is on the right and its cob web-like retreat on the left. The spider has a "sensing line" which connects its retreat to its orb web and allows it to sense the vibrations of its prey. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2023)

File.Name: n322c_n316-008.jpg
Notes: 52765487943 This is spider web under a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2023)

File.Name: n323a_n323-019.jpg
Notes: 52767033341 Here is a tiny orb-weaving spider, but it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family instead of Araneidae. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides by the shape of its abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2023)

Notes: 52767439670 These are tiny liverworts (Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon. This one might be Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri), see this photo. There are having good year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2023)

File.Name: n323c_n320-032.jpg
Notes: 52767504308 Here is another shot of small Beetle (Coleoptera) on a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago, see this photo. Maybe this is the Leaf Beetle (Chrysomelidae)? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2023)

File.Name: n324a_n324-042.jpg
Notes: 52769316124 I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a fallen oak leaf today. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2023)

File.Name: n324b_n324-001.jpg
Notes: 52768504292 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in a leaf bud of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe this spider is genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen. Maybe it is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2023)

File.Name: n324c_n323-008.jpg
Notes: 52768497427 This is a Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) on shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods yesterday. I don't know if it's the true Giant Western Crane Fly (Holorusia hespera), but it seems big enough -- probably a couple of inches across. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2023)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Giant Western Crane Fly

File.Name: n325a_n325-006.jpg
Notes: 52771058646 I found this pretty diurnal (daytime) moth (Lepidoptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's small, about 3/16 inch long, with striking metallic copper and silver colors. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, maybe genus Grapholita and species Grapholita lunatana. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2023)
CommonNames: Tortricid Moth; Canyone Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: n325b_n325-029.jpg
Notes: 52771522398 I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon today. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major). You can see its proboscis is forked and it is collecting water from the wet rock. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2022)

Notes: 52771007741 This is a tiny spider wrapping its prey on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. File.Name: n326a_n326-027.jpg
Notes: 52773836898 Here is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) near leaf buds of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe they are active in fall and spring, with a little winter gap when they just hang out and chill -- literally. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. Here is a Chaparral Camel Cricket tucked into an empty acorn cap in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2023)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Chaparral Camel Cricket

File.Name: n331a_n331-025.jpg
Notes:
52783826962 Here are the first new flowers of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I've found this year. These are the male pollen-bearing staminate catkins. The female pistillate flowers look like tiny acorns and appear later in the spring, see this photo and this photo. In years past, I've found the first flowers as early as mid-January and as late as late-March. I don't expect the main flowering until April -- whole trees will change color as catkins and new leaves appear, very beautiful. These trees support much life in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2023)

There are also a few tiny wasp galls on the catkins! I believe they are the spring galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, which will make different summer galls after they mature, see this photo and this photo at BugGuide. The galls are only a few mm long. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: n402c_n402-034.jpg
Notes: 52789080534 Here are a couple of Fluted Black Elfin Saddle mushrooms in the garden today, distinctive with a fluted stem and dark cap. I've always called identified this mushroom as Helvella lacunosa, but that species has now been split up. This one might be Helvella dryophila which is associated with hardwoods like the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I find something very similar in Oregon under conifers, see this photo on the left. I call these "brain mushrooms" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. Sometimes when you first touch the cap, you can see a little puff of spores being released. This is their usual time, towards the end of the mushroom season. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2023)

File.Name: n410c_n409-031.jpg
Notes: 52808401689 Here is a male Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) on a grass blade in the woods yesterday. The female ticks are larger and look different with a shield-like structure on their back, which has something to do with the fact that the males do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't -- mosquitoes and deer flies are similar. This raises the question what this one is doing "questing" with its arms out ready to grab onto something (or someone). It's my impression that the adult males don't feed but climb on to a host animal to find a female. These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us -- see this photo! Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2023)
