Notes: 52835002472 This is looking close of a Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago -- compare this photo. I don't think it's the true Giant Western Crane Fly (Holorusia hespera), but it seems big enough -- probably an inch or more across. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2023)
CommonNames: Large Crane Fly; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: n421c_n418-004.jpg
Notes: 52834959327 After consulting BugGuide, I'm pretty sure handsome fly is a Golden Dung Fly (Scathophaga stercoraria) in the family Scathophagidae. It's perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) a couple of days ago. I'm not sure what it's doing in our canyon, but I guess there is dung everywhere! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2023)
CommonNames: Golden Dung Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: n422a_n422-003.jpg
Notes: 52838251744 Here is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. Looking at BugGuide, I think it might be genus Conophorus. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2023)

File.Name: n422b_n422-029.jpg
Notes: 52838210739 This is a small fly (order Diptera) on a flower of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure it just laid a egg! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2023)

File.Name: n422c_n422-008.jpg
Notes: 52837412412 It's easy to recognize a lupine (Lupinus, Fabaceae) with those "palmate leaves" like the fingers on your hand. It gets harder when you try to recognize species of these plants! Here are three different little lupines in the woods today. That's the Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) which is recognizable by its bunt-tipped leaflets. These plants grow quite luxuriously after wildfires, see this photo taken after the White Fire a few years ago in Paradise. The hairy lupine on the middle is the smallest of these, and I really don't know what it is. I've called it the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus) in the past, but I don't know if that's right. It's only a few inches tall, and it grows in disturbed places along the road in our canyon. That's the Stinging Lupine aka Nettle Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) on the right. I don't think they actually sting, but they look like they could. They are not common in our area -- except after wildfires. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2023)
CommonNames: Collar Lupine; Coulter's Lupine; Stinging Lupine; Nettle Lupine
associatedTaxa: Lupinus sparsiflorus spp inopinatus; Lupinus hirsutissimus

File.Name: m101a_m109-008.jpg
Notes: 51793200078 I believe this is an Araneus andrewsi spider with humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs. It was curled up in a plant of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae), so I used a light reflector to brighten it up. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2022)

File.Name: m101b_m101-007.jpg
Notes: 51792099992 Here is a remarkably pallid Cortinarius mushroom growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This one might be Cortinarius regalis, see iNaturalist and this photo of a similar mushroom. You can see the mushroom's "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs hanging from the stem with the rusty-red spores that fall from the gills. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2022)

File.Name: m101c_lc31-007.jpg
Notes: 51793397669 I believe this dark-spored mushroom is part of the Psathyrella longipes group, though this it one is still young and pallid. They grow alone or in small groups on debris under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. The dark spore-covered gills don't turn to ink like the genus Coprinus, and they don't grow on dung like the genus Panaeolus. The caps are "hygrophanous" - changing color as they dry out. They are attractive mushrooms albeit very fragile. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2021)

File.Name: m102a_m102-027.jpg
Notes: 51796163745 This is a handsome Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2022)

File.Name: m102b_m102-007.jpg
Notes: 51795408506 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2022)

File.Name: m102c_m101-010.jpg
Notes: 51795523068 This is another a well-camouflaged caterpillar on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods yesterday. It's maybe an inch long. I'm sure it's the larva of a Moth (Lepidoptera), but I don't know what kind. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2022)

File.Name: m103a_m103-023.jpg
Notes: 51797875141 California gold! This is a large Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom found by my neighbor in his woods today. At least I know they're out there! The fresh mushrooms are delicious, see this photo. According to my records, I've found Chanterelles as early as November and as late as May, with a peak in February. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2022)

File.Name: m103b_m103-018.jpg
Notes: 51796907097 Here are new flowers of Nuttall's Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family, the first flowers I've seen this year. The flowers are tiny, about 1/10 inch. These plants climb over other shrubs, California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in this case. It ends up with small white berries, see this photo from a few years ago. Plants in this genus are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have a number of different species with tiny 4-merous flowers that all look the same, and they aren't easy to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds. They are one of my favorites - even if I get confused by them. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2022)

File.Name: m103c_m103-015.jpg
Notes: 51797941718 This is the first open flower I've found this year of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's growing in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, white balance is what it is. They are beautiful flowers, more elegant than our usual yellow "wild mustards" that are mostly non-native. They are flowering early this year. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but please don't dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2022)

File.Name: m104a_m104-009.jpg
Notes: 51800145761 This is a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly soaking up sun in the canyon today. This is one of a group of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days - including the Mourning Cloak and California Tortoiseshell. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2022)

File.Name: m104b_m104-016.jpg
Notes: 51799183077 This is a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae ) near a flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2022)

File.Name: m104c_m103-007.jpg
Notes: 51800244183 Here is a little ant (Formicidae) looking for nectar in new flower buds of This is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) under a new leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2022) (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2022)
CommonNames: ant; Hammock Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: m105a_m105-004.jpg
Notes: 51802481168 This is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on the window sill in our house this morning, see my previous photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2022)

File.Name: m105b_lc30-002.jpg
Notes: 51801389622 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on the window sill in our house last week, see my next photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2021)

File.Name: m105c_lc31-029.jpg
Notes: 51803046445 I think this is the web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods a few days ago. I think it was interrupted by the rain last week. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2021)

File.Name: m106a_m106-011.jpg
Notes: 51804570636 This is a Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly soaking up sun in the canyon today, the first one I photographed this season. This is one of a group of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days - including the Red Admiral and California Tortoiseshell. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2022)

File.Name: m106b_m102-029.jpg
Notes: 51803600897 This is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2022)

File.Name: m106c_m102-002.jpg
Notes: 51804553106 This is of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2022)

File.Name: m107a_m106-023.jpg
Notes: 51806889699 Here are two shots of a fly (Diptera) ovipositing on on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. I believe it is a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae - with long legs, hairy wings with reduced venation, and long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment that are barely visible here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2022)

File.Name: m107b_m106-006.jpg
Notes: 51807245940 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. I don't know what kind of fly it is. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2022)

File.Name: m107c_m102-016.jpg
Notes: 51805549632 This is a tiny Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae) on a leaf blade of Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods. I usually of find them dancing in the sunbeams above the oak trees, see this photo. The male has feathery antennae like this, the female less so - see this photo. BugGuide says their larvae are mostly aquatic, but we have little standing water in the mountains yet. BugGuide goes on to say that "a few [species] occur in decaying matter, under bark or in moist ground. Larvae of some species tolerate seasonal desiccation." I guess ours is one of those. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2022)

File.Name: m108a_m108-019.jpg
Notes: 51809395325 This is female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) on a old leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2022)

File.Name: m108b_m108-010.jpg
Notes: 51809006144 I believe this another shot of the Araneus andrewsi spider with humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs, see this photo. It was curled up in a plant of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) with a web nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2022)

File.Name: m108c_m106-002.jpg
Notes: 51808755553 This is another shot of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, see this photo. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2022)

File.Name: m109a_m109-007.jpg
Notes: 51811268798 Here is tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It looks like it's blowing hot jet fuel from its butt! It's tiny, maybe 1/8 inch long or less. This might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2022)

File.Name: m109b_m109-001.jpg
Notes: 51811868530 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods today. Look close - the spider has a tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) for its prey. I've tried to photograph this spider this several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2022)
CommonNames: Funnel Web Weaver; Chaparral Camel Cricket

File.Name: m109c_m104-010.jpg
Notes: 51811100876 This is of Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods a few days ago. The photo on the right is looking at the rear end on the other side of its web. In the left hand photo the spider is retrieving its prey - a tiny Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae, Diptera), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2022)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Non-biting Midge Fly

File.Name: m110a_m110-009.jpg
Notes: 51812461322 Here is a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly perched for just a moment on a leaf of in the oak mulch in the woods today. I've been seeing these butterflies out of the corner of my eye for some time as they fly by, but this is the first one I've managed to get close to. These butterflies are distinctive in having no distinctive markings below even when fresh. This is the first new butterfly I've seen this year that is not overwintering. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2022)

File.Name: m110b_m110-015.jpg
Notes: 51813395966 These are two shots of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods today. It was retrieving its prey, a tiny Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae, Diptera). This is a male with "big palps" - compare my previous photo of a female. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2022)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Non-biting Midge Fly

File.Name: m110c_m110-005.jpg
Notes: 51813727144 This is a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods today. These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. This is a female with slender palps, compare my next photo of a male. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2022)

File.Name: m111a_m111-002.jpg
Notes: 51816189705 This is a caterpillar on a leaf of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I'm sure it's the larva of a Moth (Lepidoptera), but I don't know what kind. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2022)

File.Name: m111b_m111-018.jpg
Notes: 51815455391 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods today. It was munching its prey, a tiny Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae, Diptera). This is a male with remarkably big palps. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2022)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Non-biting Midge Fly

File.Name: m111c_m111-008.jpg
Notes: 51814488162 This is a new flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods. They are flowering early this year. I'm sure it is flowering early because of the October rain we had and the warm weather that followed. I've also seen Hummingbird Sage, Bitter Gooseberry, California Bay, Eastwood Manzanita, and even Bigpod Ceanothus flowering early this year. It's disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2022)

File.Name: m112a_m112-009.jpg
Notes: 51817636714 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods today. It appears to be another male with remarkably big palps. These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2022)

File.Name: m112b_m111-013.jpg
Notes: 51817629249 Here is spider webbing on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) yesterday. I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2022)

File.Name: m112c_m107-005.jpg
Notes: 51817989905 This is the web of an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2022)

File.Name: m113a_m113-025.jpg
Notes: 51819065276 This is the first new caterpillar I've seen this year of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example see this photo from last summer. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor for late summer and winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. Adult butterflies and caterpillars were scarce last summer, and I hope they do better this year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2022)

File.Name: m113b_m113-009.jpg
Notes: 51819385059 This is a male Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. The tuning fork-shaped mark on its carapace and serrated pattern on its back are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2022)

File.Name: m113c_m112-006.jpg
Notes: 51819033351 This is female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods yesterday. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2022)

File.Name: m114a_m114-013.jpg
Notes: 51821334479 This is a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched for a moment on a sandstone outcrop. I'm pretty sure this is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera, the first I've managed to capture this year. It has brilliant red "underwings" that make them easy to spot in the air. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I seen a few flying past this winter, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. I regard these moths as almost butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2022)

File.Name: m114b_m114-004.jpg
Notes: 51821696725 Like the swallows return to Capistrano, the Bee Flies have returned to San Marcos Pass! I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) looking at its shadow on a fallen oak leaf today. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early. I've been waiting for these flies that usually appear in our woods around New Year's. I don't know where they've been, but I'm glad they're here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2022)

File.Name: m114c_m112-036.jpg
Notes: 51821081328 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I don't know what kind of fly it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2022)

File.Name: m115a_m114-014.jpg
Notes: 51823043573 This is another shot of a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched for a moment on a sandstone outcrop, see this photo from yesterday. I'm pretty sure this is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. It has brilliant red "underwings" that make them easy to spot in the air. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2022)

File.Name: m115b_m113-031.jpg
Notes: 51823260054 This is tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider under a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. It has a dark featureless ball called a "bolus" - see this photo from Ash on "Spider Teeth". (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2022)

File.Name: m115c_m113-033.jpg
Notes: 51823630860 Here is a tiny fly (Diptera) on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. I believe it is a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae - with long legs, hairy wings with reduced venation, and long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment that are barely visible here. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2022)

File.Name: m116a_m116-017.jpg
Notes: 51825929340 Here is another hand-sized Banana Slug in the woods today. If you look close at its head, you can see that it's munching the leaf of a seedling. Slugs have teeth?? Look this this photo! These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. This is probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. They are something that the rain brings, and they seem to disappear between storms. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2022)

File.Name: m116b_m116-031.jpg
Notes: 51824241342 I believe this another Araneus andrewsi spider wrapping its prey. It has humps on its back, dark colors, and bristly legs, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2022)

File.Name: m116c_m116-009.jpg
Notes: 51825265488 This is the first new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family that I've seen this year, in our canyon at home. They are flowering early this year, compare this photo from the first flower of 2018 on April 28. I'm sure it is flowering early because of the October rain we had and the warm weather that followed. I've also seen Wedge-leaved Horkelia, Hummingbird Sage, Bitter Gooseberry, California Bay, Eastwood Manzanita, and even Bigpod Ceanothus flowering early this year. It's disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2022)

File.Name: m117a_m117-017.jpg
Notes: 51827263126 Here is a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) looking at me from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. My photo gives a good view of this predator's spike-like proboscis, and you can see the little recess on the front of its thorax that the tip of its proboscis fits into when traveling. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2022)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: m117b_m116-023.jpg
Notes: 51827590899 This caterpillar looks like it's going for a ride in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's the caterpillar that will become a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in May or June if it makes it - but not all do, see this photo of a casualty. I've found several different kinds of insects and spiders in these old seed capsules, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2022)

File.Name: m117c_m116-006.jpg
Notes: 51827350443 Here is a galled flower of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I say they are galled flowers and not fruit, but they must be the galled ovaries of flowers - see this photo. That odd beaked shape is typical. I'm pretty sure these are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2022)

File.Name: m118a_m118-042.jpg
Notes: 51829221996 Here are a pair of tiny mating flies on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe they are Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae, Diptera). This is real sexual dimorphism, meaning that males and females look totally different, see this photo for another example with crab spiders. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2022)

File.Name: m118b_m118-025.jpg
Notes: 51829314443 This the Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods today. We had a bit of rain last night, and it sought shelter under and old leaf of Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's a same spider male with remarkably big palps that I showed in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2022)

File.Name: m118c_m118-028.jpg
Notes: 51829194511 Here is a tiny male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2022)

File.Name: m119a_m119-032.jpg
Notes: 51831422439 This is a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) with prey under an oak leaf today. I believe it's captured a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2022)

File.Name: m119b_m119-027.jpg
Notes: 51831174488 Here is a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I can't make out what I'm seeing - maybe the shed skin (aka exuviae) after recently molting? (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2022)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: m119c_m118-008.jpg
Notes: 51830100487 This is a spider web after a small rain. The plant is Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2022)

File.Name: m120a_m120-003.jpg
Notes: 51832939893 This is another female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods yesterday. You can clearly see her darkened epigynum on the screen-right end of her abdomen, her organ for receiving the genetic material of a male. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2022)

File.Name: m120b_m120-020.jpg
Notes: 51833557590 Here is a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2022)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: m120c_m120-012.jpg
Notes: 51831876042 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods today. It appears to be another male with remarkably big palps. These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2022)

File.Name: m121a_m121-021.jpg

File.Name: m122b_m122-025.jpg
Notes: 51835950357 This is a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example see this photo from last summer. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor for late summer and winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. Adult butterflies and caterpillars were scarce last summer, and I hope they do better this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2022)

Notes: 51846991341 Here is a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2022)

File.Name: m127c_m120-023.jpg
Notes: 51847629845 Here is a male Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) on a grass blade. The female ticks are larger and look different with a shield-like structure on their back, which has something to do with the fact that the males do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't - mosquitoes and deer flies are similar. This raises the question what this one is doing at the end of the grass stem if not to hook on to a host. Well, maybe it's looking for a mate rather than a meal? These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo! Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2022)

File.Name: m128a_m128-008.jpg
Notes: 51849142614 This is a Satyr Comma (Polygonia satyrus) on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. It has its proboscis out - no doubt trying to get water from the rock. Sandstone holds a lot of water after a rain, but there's probably not much left after our good rain last month. I've never seen one of these before. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2022)

File.Name: m128b_m128-005.jpg
Notes:
51847824787 These are the light-trails of tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams. I guess they are tiny Midges in the family Chironomidae, see this photo and this photo. I assume this is a mating thing. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2022)

These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so - close, but not too close. Then the trick to getting these photos is to slow your camera down. I got this photo at 0.4 sec @ f/20 @ ISO 100, on a tripod of course. Some post-processing to bump up the contrast helps. In some years, we have large mating swarms of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees - see this photo from three years ago. You'd think the flies would be an easy target for birds and other predators, but it's like swimming into a school of anchovies - they're all around but impossible to touch.

Notes: 51849884217 This is a Satyr Comma (Polygonia satyrus) on a sandstone outcrop in the woods yesterday, see this photo. If you get close, you can see the "comma" on its hindwing. I've never seen one of these before. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2022)

File.Name: m130a_m130-013.jpg
Notes: 51853084896 This photo shows the pupal husk of an emerging two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) coming out of its gall on native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I first noticed something similar in this photo. I did some research and found this on page 61 of Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) describing the ways of Gall Wasps (Cecidomyiidae): ". . . the mature pupae exit only about two-thirds of the way out, using the pressure of the last part of the passage to hold them firmly in place while they complete their transformation into adults." That pretty much perfectly describes what I see in my photo. The shrubs aren't flowering yet, is this a problem for the gall midge? (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2022)

File.Name: m130b_m129-031.jpg
Notes: 51852098247 These are the first new flowers of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) that I've seen this year. The grown-together "connate leaves" that surround the stems make a delicious salad. The plant is kind of a weed in our garden - and around the world. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2022)

File.Name: m130c_m129-012.jpg
Notes: 51853708550 This is of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the canyon today. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2022)

File.Name: m131a_m131-028.jpg
Notes: 51855880335 Here is a debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect on leaves of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, less than 1/8 inch. Apparently these debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter other predators - though I don't know what they find in woods that is so white. It's great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look for legs! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2022)
CommonNames: debris-carrying larva; Green Lacewing; Wild Sweetpea

CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Coast Live Oak; Sawfly Wasp

File.Name: m201a_m201-021.jpg
Notes: 51856180982 I believe this a new flower of the Spreading Larkspur (Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, Ranunculaceae) in the woods today. They are flowering early this year, compare this photo taken of 16 April 2019. I'm sure it is flowering early because of the October rain we had and the warm weather that followed. I've also seen Canyon Sunflower, Wedge-leaved Horkelia, Hummingbird Sage, Bitter Gooseberry, California Bay, Eastwood Manzanita, and even Bigpod Ceanothus flowering early this year. It's disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2022)

File.Name: m201b_m129-028.jpg
Notes: 51856166237 I'm pretty sure that this is a line of Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) eggs on a stem of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The eggs look like little beans, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2022)

File.Name: m201c_m127-005.jpg
Notes: 51857769000 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2022)

File.Name: m202a_m202-035.jpg
Notes:
51859190159 It's curious that both male and female trees of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek picked today to open their first flowers. These trees are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and the ladies look quite different from the gentlemen. That's the male pollen-bearing staminate catkin. These catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many insects that will hopefully soon appear. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2022)

Today is Groundhog Day, midway between solstice and equinox, and we are getting more light in our winter-shady canyon. If any ground-dwelling gopher stuck its head out today, it definitely saw its shadow - it was sunny and clear. By tradition, that means six more weeks of winter - but do you really trust your weather to rodents?? The NWS long-range outlook doesn't look so good.

File.Name: m206b_m206-023.jpg
Notes: 51866769208 I was staring down when a I suddenly noticed this snake looking back at me. It was quite still, waiting to see what I would do next. I believe it's a California Striped Racer (Coluber lateralis lateralis) aka Chaparral Whipsnake (Masticophis lateralis lateralis). I don't often notice snakes, so it was a pleasure to spot this one. It's not large, maybe 2 or 3 feet long, fast and slender. Once I noticed it, the snake made its getaway - but not before I got the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2022)

File.Name: m208a_m208-012.jpg
Notes: 51871379585 Me and my shadow. . . . This is handsome California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. It has its proboscis out - no doubt trying to get water from the rock. Sandstone holds a lot of water after a rain, but there's probably not much left after our good rain in December - the moss and liverworts on the same outcrop have mostly dried out already to wait for the next rain. This butterfly is part of a group that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days like today. I rarely see them in summer. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2022)

CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Black Sage; Labyrinth Spider

File.Name: m213b_m213-004.jpg
Notes: 51880660849 Some critter was impatient waiting for this flower of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) to open, so it made its own hole to get inside! Lots of insects practice nectar theft, entering a flower through the calyx or a hole rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen, see this photo of a different manzanita. This insect blasted out the whole wall of the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2022)

File.Name: m213c_m213-026.jpg
Notes: 51879372052 This is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) under a new leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2022)

File.Name: m214a_m214-007.jpg
Notes: 51881489757 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on an old seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider will into one of the little seed pockets! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2022)

File.Name: m214b_m214-015.jpg
Notes: 51881483407 This is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) with prey under an oak leaf today. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2022)

File.Name: m214c_m214-016.jpg
Notes: 51882526253 Here is another shot of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2022)

File.Name: m215a_m215-027.jpg
Notes: 51884446478 Here is another shot of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. If you look close at the space between the spider's abdomen and cephalothorax, you can see that it's carrying a passenger - I'll make a note. I believe this is the larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera. I know they can move around, so they're not attached like ticks. I've seen these passengers before, see this photo and this photo. There are large mantidflies like the Wasp Mantidfly in this photo, but I assume this one will become something smaller like the one in this photo. I really only know what I've seen for myself about these interesting insects. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2022)

File.Name: m215b_m215-039.jpg
Notes: 51884363136 This is tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) spider in a leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2022)

File.Name: m215c_m215-019.jpg
Notes: 51884661049 These are new flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) which are just starting to open up here in the mountains, though they've been going for some time at lower elevations. The new flowers are intricate, like little pentagonal origami boxes with secrets inside - probably thrips and tiny beetles. Other species are deeper blue, but we still have to wait for those - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2022)

File.Name: m216a_m216-018.jpg
Notes: 51886549514 This is a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) with prey on pollen-bearing staminate catkins of a Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek today. It's prey are mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera). (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2022)
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Arroyo Willow; Dance Flies

File.Name: m216b_m213-021.jpg
Notes: 51886215711 Here is the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae). It consists of three main parts. The thickest part in my photo is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is random webbing that supports the bowl and might knock down flying insects into it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2022)

File.Name: m216c_m211-022.jpg
Notes: 51885217402 Here is a tiny Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider with a large web on an old seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider is quite small, less than 1/8 inch, maybe a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), see closer shot this photo. It also looks like the work of a Zygiella or Parazygiella with a missing section like a pie slice? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2022)

File.Name: m217a_m217-004.jpg
Notes: 51888453819 This is another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on a stem of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2022)

File.Name: m217b_m217-027.jpg
Notes: 51888109426 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods today. It appears to be another male with big palps. These tiny spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2022)

File.Name: m217c_m211-026.jpg
Notes: 51888178108 Here is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. This is the wasp that has its abdomen on a stalk or petiole. They mostly stay at their nests in mid-winter, but it was warm enough last week that they were out and about. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2022)

File.Name: m218a_m218-015.jpg
Notes: 51889036182 Here is a chubby little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Looking at BugGuide, I think it might be genus Conophorus. I don't know anything else about it. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2022)

File.Name: m218b_m218-030.jpg
Notes: 51890639115 This is another mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on male pollen-bearing staminate catkin of a Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek today. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the catkins. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2022)

File.Name: m218c_m218-004.jpg
Notes: 51889983086 I believe this is a Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides) in the family Pholcidae catching a beam of sunlight in the corner of a window inside our house. I usually find these little long-legged spiders on our windows, or walking across the ceiling, or in the sink or shower, or in my empty coffee cup in the morning just before I pour . . . pretty much everywhere inside, there must be 100s of them all told! Their legs look like they're made of capillary glass tubing. Apparently they are introduced from Central Europe - but they sure have made themselves at home here. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2022)

File.Name: m219a_m219-014.jpg
Notes: 51892448250 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2022)

File.Name: m219b_m219-006.jpg
Notes: 51892116644 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on leaf of Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It might be an Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae. Those golden hairs on its thorax are distinctive - sometimes they make a real fur coat like in this photo from a couple of years ago, but they shed away as they age. This is one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2022)

File.Name: m219c_m217-022.jpg
Notes: 51891854773 Here is an ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) shepherding it's flock of tiny aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2022)

File.Name: m220a_m220-029.jpg
Notes: 51894043423 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) waiting at the end of a grass stem in the woods today. I guess the tick is a Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis). This tick is small, only about 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2022)

File.Name: m220b_m220-012.jpg
Notes: 51894010213 This is a the work of little caterpillar on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The caterpillar is absent. The caterpillar is skeletonizing the leaf and turning the leaf material into frass. It's interesting that it has covered its work area on the bottom of the leaf with a clear material like plastic food wrap, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2022)

File.Name: m220c_m220-024.jpg
Notes: 51894253914 Here is another shot of Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2022)

File.Name: m221a_m221-011.jpg
Notes: 51895167502 This is a small larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. Sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots and always have six or more pairs of prolegs - see this photo for more info. Despite being wasps rather than caterpillars, they have a great appetite for foliage - Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in this case. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2022)

File.Name: m221b_m221-004.jpg
Notes: 51896460954 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods today. It appears to be another male with big palps. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2022)

File.Name: m221c_m220-006.jpg
Notes: 51895145387 Here is tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods yesterday. It's tiny, maybe 1/8 inch long or less. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2022)

File.Name: m222a_m222-021.jpg
Notes: 51898619899 I found this tiny spider on the plexiglass windshield of the little electric cart I use to get around in our canyon today. This is a small Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae). I wonder what it was doing in my cart? (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2022)

File.Name: m222b_m222-009.jpg
Notes: 51898601404 This are the first new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family that I've seen this year in our canyon at home. They are flowering early this year, compare this photo from the first flowers of 2014 on May 2. I'm sure they are flowering early because of the October rain we had and the warm weather that followed. It's disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2022)

File.Name: m222c_m222-005.jpg
Notes: 51898575189 These are new flowers of the Spreading Larkspur (Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, Ranunculaceae) in the woods today. They are flowering early this year, compare this photo taken of 16 April 2019. I'm sure it is flowering early because of the October rain we had and the warm weather that followed. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2022)

File.Name: m223a_m223-009.jpg
Notes: 51900692984 This is a long-legged Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's the first of these proper Crane Flies with long palps that I've seen this year. I don't know if it's the true Giant Western Crane Fly (Holorusia hespera), but it seems big enough - probably a couple of inches across. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2022)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Giant Western Crane Fly

File.Name: m223b_m223-004.jpg
Notes: 51900348026 This is a web of Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on the window sill in our house this morning. If you look close in the funnel opening, you can see the spider! Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2022)

File.Name: m223c_m218-006.jpg
Notes: 51900328751 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on the window sill in our house last week. It has little moth for prey. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2022)

File.Name: m224a_m224-001.jpg
Notes: 51902868945 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods today. These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2022)

File.Name: m224b_m223-022.jpg
Notes: 51901251967 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. These wasps have no sign of a "wasp-waist" unlike most of their cousins. They are just starting to appear this year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2022)

File.Name: m224c_m220-004.jpg
Notes: 51902530624 This is Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on its web in the woods - at least the rear end. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2022)

File.Name: m225a_m225-009.jpg
Notes: 51904571574 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on leaf of Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fly it is, maybe it's another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of all all the bristles. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2022)

File.Name: m225b_m225-004.jpg
Notes: 51904226866 This is a Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae) with a small fly (Diptera) beside it - that's all the scale you need! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2022)

File.Name: m225c_m223-012.jpg
Notes: 51904850095 This is looking at the remarkable face of a Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family hiding in a shrub of native on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) near the creek - see this photo for (nearly) the whole fly. You can clearly see in my photo that these large flies have the terminal segment of their palps longer than the other segments combined - those dangly bits at the end of its head. That makes them family Tipulidae. We also have smaller Crane Flies in the same group of Tipulomorpha that differ in having the terminal segment of their palps short, compare this photo of (probably) a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2022)
CommonNames: Large Crane Fly; Coast Live Oak; Crane Flies

File.Name: m226a_m226-018.jpg
Notes: 51906283058 A friend gave us a pupa of a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) butterfly and it sat in our kitchen for 2-1/2 weeks. Suddenly there was a butterfly! It emerged from the chrysalis yesterday and spent in the night in our greenhouse. It's still there. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2022)

File.Name: m226b_m225-034.jpg
Notes: 51905201827 A friend gave us a pupa of a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) butterfly and it sat in our kitchen for 2-1/2 weeks. Suddenly there was a butterfly! The numbers refer to the date the caterpillar pupated. It emerged from the chrysalis yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2022)

File.Name: m226c_m225-031.jpg
Notes: 51906796780 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Other plants in the mountains don't even have flower buds yet, a sign of the odd water year we're having. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2022)

File.Name: m227a_m227-002.jpg
Notes: 51908518098 This is a mosquito-sized Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) on leaf a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I don't believe the fly is a proper Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family because it doesn't have the long palps that those flies have - compare this photo for an example. I think it might be a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae. I suppose it might also be a Winter Crane Fly (Trichoceridae), though I don't see the ocelli that distinguish that family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2022)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Limoniid Crane Fly; Winter Crane Fly

File.Name: m227a_m227-029.jpg
Notes: 51908399621 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) on a on leaf of Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It might be a Leaf Miner Fly (Agromyzidae). It appears to be ovipositing. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2022)

File.Name: m227b_m227-012.jpg
Notes: 51908999235 Here are Bowl and Doily Spiders (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) that are sharing a web together. I suspect that's the male on the left and a larger female on the right. You can see them both together on see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2022)

File.Name: m228a_m228-012.jpg
Notes: 51910664778 Here is another shot of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. If you look close at the space between the spider's abdomen and cephalothorax, you can see that it's carrying a passenger - I'll make a note. I believe this is the larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera, see this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2022)

File.Name: m228b_m228-016.jpg
Notes: 51910556251 This is a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) with galls along the edges. They are one of several different kinds of "rolled leaf gall" that we seem to have on our trees, the one on the left in this photo. I'm sure they are proper galls and not the work of leaf-roller insects because the cellular structure of the leaf has been changed and not just mechanically folded and tied down. I know nothing else about these galls as they are not listed in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2022)

File.Name: m228c_m228-024.jpg
Notes: 51910540516 This is a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I believe contains a colony of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae. It's a "rolled leaf gall" that always folds up onto the top of the leaf which distinguishes it from the galled leaf in my [Next] photo which fold down over the underside of the leaf. You can see a some of the tiny aphids, I'll make a note. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2022)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: m301a_m301-020.jpg
Notes: 51913305495 Here is a tiny Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on an old seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider is quite small, less than 1/8 inch. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, compare this photo and this photo. It's got a hint of a cat face on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2022)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Black Sage; Labyrinth Spider

File.Name: m301b_m301-006.jpg
Notes: 51913285755 I found this tiny spider on the plexiglass windshield of the little electric cart I use to get around in our canyon today, see this photo. This is a small Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) with a fly for prey. It was turning its prey around and around while pumping its abdomen rhythmically. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2022)

File.Name: m301c_m301-030.jpg
Notes: 51912980069 This is a work of a leaf-miner caterpillar on leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2022)

File.Name: m302a_m302-007.jpg
Notes: 51913747437 This is the small web of an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2022)

File.Name: m302b_m302-003.jpg
Notes: 51914696526 Here are signs of a struggle on the web of an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the woods this morning. Maybe the victim was a moth with wing scales? The web is about the size of a volleyball, but there was no sign of the spider. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2022)

File.Name: m302c_m228-026.jpg
Notes: 51915009554 This is webbing between leaves on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I believe it's the web of caterpillar and not a spider. If you look close, you can see insect frass (poop) caught up in the webbing and spiders don't make that stuff. I guess this is the work of a little moth caterpillar, but I have yet to see it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2022)

File.Name: m303a_m303-027.jpg
Notes: 51916634101 Here is a nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Orthoptera) perched over the throat of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny about 1/8 inch. This nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2022)

File.Name: m303b_m303-017.jpg
Notes: 51915646707 I found this "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it's some kind of Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae. There are many different kinds, so 'nuff said. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2022)

File.Name: m303c_m303-009.jpg
Notes: 51916702478 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods today - at least the rear end. It appears to be another male with big palps. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2022)

File.Name: m304a_m304-020.jpg
Notes: 51918438501 Here is a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. Maybe the bee is genus Anthophora or Habropoda in the family Apidae? (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2022)

File.Name: m304b_m301-002.jpg
Notes: 51918520368 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) caught in a spider web. I found this fly on the plexiglass windshield of the little electric cart I use to get around in our canyon. The web belongs to a small Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae), see this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2022)

File.Name: m304c_m228-008.jpg
Notes: 51918415286 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a small oak branch in the woods. I don't know what kind of fly it is, maybe it's another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of all all the bristles. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2022)

File.Name: m305a_m305-007.jpg
Notes:
51920447006 Here is a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) that I believe is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae - their shape is pretty distinctive. I found it on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) that is growing on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The wasp is tiny, about 1/8 inch. I have hundreds of photos of different wasp galls but few of the wasps themselves, see this album. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2022)

I have a few other photos that might be gall wasps: here, here, here, here, here, here, and here.
CommonNames: Gall Wasp; Wild Sweetpea; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: m305b_m305-004.jpg
Notes: 51920760854 You can always recognize a lupine by its palmate leaves - like the open fingers of your hand. This one is a native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus, Fabaceae) growing in our canyon at home, with those distinctive skinny truncate leaflets. I think of it as a fire-follower, but it's happy on disturbed roadsides as well. It's actually one of my favorites, though it's less dramatic than some lupines. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2022)

File.Name: m305c_m303-042.jpg
Notes: 51920747819 This is a different gall on a leaf bud of a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It reminds me of the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae, Cynipidae), but there are differences - see this photo. The colors are different, it seems more hairy, and the way the bracts hold the gall like a claw and ball foot is also different. Both galls mature quickly and drop away. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2022)

File.Name: m306a_m306-002.jpg
Notes: 51923037059 This is another tunnel of a leaf-miner caterpillar meandering through a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can follow the path of the caterpillar's life from a small start to a big finish, growing as it moves along. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2022)

File.Name: m306b_m306-010.jpg
Notes: 51923300140 Here are Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) plants with a background of California Liverwort (Asterella californica) plants that have faded to black on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. There are also little still-green seedlings that is not a liverwort - Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) and Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma, Brassicaceae). The liverworts aren't dead, but they head "back to the shadows again" when the sandstone starts to dry out. They are real "resurrection plants" that will return with more rain. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2022)
CommonNames: Bush Monkeyflower; California Liverwort; Miner's Lettuce; Bitter Cress
associatedTaxa: Asterella californica; Claytonia perfoliata; Cardamine oligosperma

File.Name: m306c_m305-011.jpg
Notes: 51922998354 Here is another shot of the tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) that I believe is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae - their shape is pretty distinctive. I found it on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) that is growing on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2022)
CommonNames: Gall Wasp; Wild Sweetpea; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: m307a_m307-004.jpg
Notes: 51925088778 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fly it is. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2022)

File.Name: m307b_m307-015.jpg
Notes: 51924984196 This are tiny aphids (Aphididae) on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2022)

File.Name: m307c_m307-019.jpg
Notes: 51924969181 Here are new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2022)

File.Name: m308a_m308-018.jpg
Notes: 51927352434 Here is gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I've seen a few of these butterflies this year, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. I like this one because it shows the butterfly's pollen-encrusted proboscis, and I'm sure that it's been sipping Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). These flowers have deep throats, and this is how the butterflies move pollen - and pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2022)

File.Name: m308b_m308-014.jpg
Notes: 51927629280 This is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly working new flowers of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The Mournful and Funereal Duskywings are similar - with addition of white trim on their hindwings, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2022)

File.Name: m308c_m308-021.jpg
Notes: 51926017312 Here is a nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Orthoptera) on Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, about 1/8 inch. This nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2022)

File.Name: m309a_m308-033.jpg
Notes: 51929594345 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday - ovipositing? These wasps have no sign of a "wasp-waist" unlike most of their cousins. They are just starting to appear this year - at the same time that the oaks are forming catkins, see this photo. Sometimes the larvae of these wasps become pests and attack the new leaves and catkins of oak trees, see this photo. They don't seem to do this every year, so there's some mystery here. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2022)

File.Name: m309b_m308-009.jpg
Notes: 51928957311 Here is the another web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae). It consists of three main parts. The thickest part in my photo is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is random webbing that supports the bowl and might knock down flying insects into it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2022)

File.Name: m309c_m307-012.jpg
Notes: 51929579375 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods. These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2022)

File.Name: m310b_m310-004.jpg
Notes: 51931203054 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Notes: 51931746502 This is a stunning Bramble Hairstreak aka Lotus Hairstreak aka Coastal Green Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly ovipositing on a leaf of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. The name "Bramble Hairstreak" is really a misnomer as these butterflies have nothing to do with brambles except for being green. They are beautiful butterflies, and I hope to find more this year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2022)
CommonNames: Bramble Hairstreak; Lotus Hairstreak; Coastal Green Hairstreak

CommonNames: Bramble Hairstreak; Lotus Hairstreak; Coastal Green Hairstreak; Deerweed

File.Name: m313a_m313-003.jpg
Notes: 51937085504 This is another larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. Sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots and always have six or more pairs of prolegs - see this photo for more info. Despite being wasps rather than caterpillars, they have a great appetite for foliage - Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in this case. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2022)

CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

I don't run into these handsome insects very often, but I do seem to find one or more in most years. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2022)

51951068338 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) that happened to be in the center of a new lupine leaf. It's a Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I don't know what kind of fly it is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2022)

File.Name: m320b_m320-017.jpg
Notes: 51949988582 This is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) that had fallen prey to an entomopathogenic fungus -- a fungus that a parasite that kills its host. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2022)

File.Name: m321a_m321-006.jpg
Notes: 51953300863 Here is a debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, less than 1/8 inch. Apparently these debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter other predators - though I don't know what they find in woods that is so white. It's great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look for legs! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2022)

Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2022)

Notes: 51957623154 Here is the another web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae). It consists of three main parts. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is random webbing that supports the bowl and might knock down flying insects into it. File.Name: m323b_m323-012.jpg
Notes: 51956311117 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in last year's plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. The spider's orb web is on the right and its cob web-like retreat on the left. The spider has a "sensing line" which connects its retreat to its orb web and allows it to sense the vibrations of its prey. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2022)

If you look close, you can see insect frass (poop) caught up in the webbing and spiders don't make that stuff. I guess this is the work of a little moth caterpillar, but I have yet to see it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2022)

Notes: 51959780130 This is a "Jerusalem Cricket" aka "Potato Bug" (Ammopelmatus fuscus aka Stenopelmatus fuscus, family Stenopelmatidae, Tettigoniidea, Ensifera, Orthoptera) under a stone in the our garden. That long string of Latin names is actually interesting because it tells that this critter is more closely related to katydids (Tettigoniidea) than true crickets (Gryllidea), see -- compare my next two photos. It's huge, about two inches. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2022)

File.Name: m328a_m328-013.jpg
Notes: 51967784523 Here is a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden today -- with raindrops from the rain today. I first found these caterpillars last summer, see this photo. They will feed into the summer, and then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults later this year. This shrub is a "scroph" too, along with the more typical Bush Monkey Flower and Climbing Penstemon that they usually favor. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2022)
