Notes: 51972470274 This is a retreat of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in last year's old seed head of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The tiny spider is in there, see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2022)

File.Name: m331a_m331-041.jpg
Notes: 51974332673 This is a little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). The fly is female, I can clearly see her ovipositor, and I suspect she's about to oviposit on the flower. One possibility for ID is the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2022)
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Sunflower Seed Maggot

File.Name: m331b_m331-008.jpg
Notes: 51974805475 I found this caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a shrub of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, compare this photo. It is beautiful and fairly large, about an inch long. I'm not sure whose larva it is. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2022)

File.Name: m331c_m328-028.jpg
Notes: 51974524334 This is another shot for a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) ovipositing -- laying eggs -- an a group of aphids (family Aphididae) on Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2022)

File.Name: m401a_m401-031.jpg
Notes: 51976668030 Here are two shots of a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly in the woods today. The photo on the right was taken on the ground. I followed it up into a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The butterfly is actually tilted towards the sun like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly day. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2022)

File.Name: m401b_m401-014.jpg
Notes: 51976086606 I found this Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) prickly pear cactus in the garden today. These handsome flies are not really common here, but I find them now and then in the woods. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2022)

File.Name: m401c_m331-039.jpg
Notes: 51975068597 This is a another Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. The fly is female, I can clearly see her ovipositor, and I suspect she's about to oviposit on the flower. One possibility for ID is the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2022)
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Sunflower Seed Maggot

File.Name: m402a_m402-010.jpg
Notes: 51977183842 I believe it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae -- superfamily Ichneumonoidea for sure. This is on of leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) that is still wet from the fog this morning. These are parasitic wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2022)

File.Name: m402b_m402-026.jpg
Notes: 51977149672 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It has psychedelic colors! It was to hard to photograph in the wind and the fog today, but I did it. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2022)

File.Name: m402c_m401-003.jpg
Notes: 51978425794 Here is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda), probably a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. It's interesting to see the snail so fully extended. This is a native snail and NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails that are so common in the lowlands. I believe they are mostly nocturnal and stay out of sight. HSS! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2022)

File.Name: m403a_m403-002.jpg
Notes: 51980865114 Here are galls growing on new spring leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure whose galls they are, but I've been calling them "Painted Toenail Galls". Hah - part of the fun of finding new galls is making up names for them! Ron Russo doesn't describe them in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) book. See this photo for particularly well-developed galls. They look different as they age, but don't we all - see this photo of an older gall which also has an exit hole, so I'm pretty sure they are made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2022)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps

File.Name: m403b_m403-015.jpg
Notes: 51979569037 I'm pretty sure this is the egg of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly of an oak leaf. I was put on the right track by recent work by Dom Greves showing White Admiral butterfly eggs in the UK, see this photo - Admirals and Sisters are both in the same Limenitidinae subfamily, and both have eggs like tiny geodesic domes. See this photo for the actual butterfly hatching. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2022)

File.Name: m403c_m402-014.jpg
Notes: 51980635048 This is another larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. Sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots and always have six or more pairs of prolegs - see this photo for more info. Despite being wasps rather than caterpillars, they have a great appetite for foliage - Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2022)

File.Name: m404a_m404-016.jpg
Notes: 51983157703 Here is a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) trying to warm on an leaf of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. Maybe it's another Mining Bee in the genus Andrena which are said to be active early in the season? (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2022)

File.Name: m404b_m404-035.jpg
Notes: 51983072441 This is a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) on a new plant of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. The cobwebby hairs are part of the plant, and give the plant its other common name of Cobweb Thistle. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2022)

File.Name: m404c_m404-005.jpg
Notes: 51983597020 I believe is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a new buds of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Or this it?? Look at it's strange "double antennae" like antlers! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2022)

File.Name: m405a_m405-037.jpg
Notes: 51985498164 This is a Bramble Hairstreak aka Lotus Hairstreak aka Coastal Green Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly in the woods today. Look close, it's ovipositing on a leaf of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2022)
CommonNames: Bramble Hairstreak; Lotus Hairstreak; Coastal Green Hairstreak; Deerweed

File.Name: m405b_m405-042.jpg
Notes: 51985757495 This is a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera in a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. They is really tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2022)

File.Name: m405c_m405-018.jpg
Notes: 51985470849 This is a tiny Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) caterpillar on a Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2022)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: m406a_m406-013.jpg
Notes: 51987338006 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2022)

File.Name: m406b_m406-024.jpg
Notes: 51987392683 This is a retreat of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in last year's plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2022)

File.Name: m406c_m406-018.jpg
Notes: 51987613809 This is a retreat of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in last year's old seed head of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2022)

File.Name: m407a_m407-012.jpg
Notes: 51989651259 Here is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly . . . on my hand! It's actually "puddling" - taking moisture and minerals from my sweat. It first landed on my right hand, and I transferred it to my left hand so I could work my camera buttons - that's how calm it was. After about ten minutes (and more than a hundred photos!) it had its fill and took off. This was a literal stretch with my 105 mm macro lens - it's much easier to do with a 55 mm lens. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2022)

File.Name: m407b_m407-019.jpg
Notes: 51989420008 This is another Bramble Hairstreak aka Lotus Hairstreak aka Coastal Green Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly in the woods today. Look close, it's ovipositing on a leaf of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2022)
CommonNames: Bramble Hairstreak; Lotus Hairstreak; Coastal Green Hairstreak; Deerweed

File.Name: m407c_m405-008.jpg
Notes: 51988340262 This the another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in a flower of a Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods. These spiders always have that odd handle on the back of its abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2022)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: m408a_m407-011.jpg
Notes: 51991615539 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family in the woods today. This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers like these, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here). The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer flower in our mountains. They are pretty common here on San Marcos Pass - both in woodland (like these) and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2022)

File.Name: m408b_m407-027.jpg
Notes: 51990323907 I believe this is a tiny Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea) in last year's plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods yesterday. It has interesting antennae and remarkable hind legs - more visible in this photo of a similar (but different) insect. These wasps are parasitoids of other insects -- BugGuide says they mostly attack Lepidoptera pupae. I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2022)

File.Name: m408c_m405-026.jpg
Notes: 51990299852 This is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on the flower head of a non-native weedy composite, probably Smooth Cat's Ear (Hypochaeris glabra, Asteraceae). The fly is probably Paragus haemorrhous. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2022)

File.Name: m409a_m409-009.jpg
Notes: 51993586283 Here is tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, maybe 1/8 inch long or less. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2022)

File.Name: m409b_m409-045.jpg
Notes: 51992498182 I believe this is a Tortoise Beetle (Cassidinae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti, Solanaceae) in woods today. Maybe it is the Eggplant Tortoise Beetle (Gratiana pallidula)? (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2022)

File.Name: m409c_m409-020.jpg
Notes: 51993476236 This is a little beetle (Coleoptera) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in woods today. I'm not sure what kind it is, but I've seen it before -- see this photo. Perhaps is the genus Trirhabda? (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2022)

File.Name: m410_m410-024.jpg
Notes: 51996496450 This is another little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera in a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. They is really tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2022)

File.Name: m410b_m410-014.jpg
Notes: 51994923297 This is a tiny grasshopper (Orthoptera) on a leaf of on Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2022)

File.Name: m410c_m409-049.jpg
Notes: 51996180769 Here is one of the first new flowers of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus, Liliaceae) in the woodland yesterday. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily. They are a "tender trap" for pollinators -- easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. They are very elegant, one of my favorite spring flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2022)

File.Name: m411a_m411-025.jpg
Notes: 51998743774 This is an "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It is a Plant Bug in the family Miridae or something similar. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2022)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Plant Bug

File.Name: m411b_m411-017.jpg
Notes: 51999004645 This is a little moth (Lepidoptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). It's small, about 3/16 inch long. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, maybe genus Grapholita and species Grapholita lunatana. Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2022)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Tortricid Moth

File.Name: m411c_m411-031.jpg
Notes: 51998998610 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) in a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I guess the tick is a Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis). This tick is small, only about 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2022)

File.Name: m412a_m412-009.jpg
Notes: 51999809422 I found this handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It is probably genus Platypedia. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that I sometimes hear up in the oaks. I don't run into these handsome insects very often, but I do seem to find one or more in most years. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2022)

File.Name: m412b_m412-002.jpg
Notes: 52000875308 This is a large Bumble Bee (Bombus) clinging onto a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It was probably trying to warm up on a cold morning. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2022)

File.Name: m412c_m411-007.jpg
Notes: 51999785607 This is a mating Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). One possibility for ID is the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2022)

File.Name: m413a_m413-031.jpg
Notes: 52003143333 Here is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira, Araneidae) in last year's plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. The spider's orb web is on the left and its cobweb-like retreat on the right. The spider has a "sensing line" which connects its retreat to its orb web and allows it to sense the vibrations of its prey. What I find most interesting is that the sensing line is under tension, actually pulling the center of the web towards the spider. I guess it carries vibrations better that way, like an old-fashioned cup and string telephone which only works if the string is tight. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2022)

File.Name: m413b_m413-020.jpg
Notes: 52003319099 Here is tiny spider on native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe the frizzy webbing is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2022)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Mesh Web Weaver

File.Name: m413c_m411-033.jpg
Notes: 52003114573 Here is spider webbing strung between native last year's plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods. I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2022)

Notes: 52005354589 This is an nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Plant Bug

Notes: 52005609790 I found this small male Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a developing seed pod of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. Its heavily spined legs give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2022)
CommonNames: Western Lynx Spider; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: m414c_m414-020.jpg
Notes: 52005138048 Here is a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) in the woods today. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2022)

File.Name: m415a_m415-006.jpg
Notes: 52007403919 This is a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2022)
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: m415b_m413-009.jpg
Notes: 52007197733 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fly it is, maybe it's another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of all all the bristles. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2022)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: m415c_m409-023.jpg
Notes: 52007639685 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) ovipositing on a flower bud on a native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods. It might be a Leaf Miner Fly (Agromyzidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2022)

File.Name: m416a_m416-032.jpg
Notes: 52009336161 This is a "true bug" on flowers of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), and it's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymph that I showed in my [Previous] photo. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I also find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana) and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus). (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2022)

File.Name: m416b_m416-038.jpg
Notes: 52009303026 Look close . . . this is not an ant but a remarkable ant mimic! It's a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that looks and moves like a real ant, but no ant has a proboscis like that. The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae), see my [Next] photo. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. This one is on Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), but I also find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana) and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus). (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2022)

File.Name: m416c_m416-002.jpg
Notes: 52009529714 I believe it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae -- superfamily Ichneumonoidea for sure. This is on of leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) this morning. These are parasitic wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2022)

Notes: 52011840059 Here is a perfect California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on our chipseal road in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2022)

Notes: 52012087220 This is a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) caterpillar on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This one has clown colors, like a circus train coming to town! If you're wondering, its head is on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2022)

File.Name: m418a_m418-026.jpg
Notes: 52013110082 This is a Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly on a new plant of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. The cobwebby hairs are part of the plant, and give the plant its other common name of Cobweb Thistle. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2022)

File.Name: m418b_m418-005.jpg
Notes: 52014367364 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It has calico colors! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2022)

File.Name: m418c_m418-002.jpg
Notes: 52014153853 This is group of tiny eggs that I found under a California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) leaf in the woods today. The eggs are suspended in a sac. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2022)

File.Name: m419a_m419-004.jpg
Notes: 52016458846 Here is a another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Orthoptera) on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. Hah, this insect was bouncing up and down in the wind today, and the photo came out better than I expected. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2022)

File.Name: m419b_m418-024.jpg
Notes: 52016430516 I found this small caterpillar on a flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in woods yesterday. I believe it's the caterpillar of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group of Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. I won't try to guess beyond that. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2022)

File.Name: m419c_m416-004.jpg
Notes: 52015388957 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods. I believe this a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina - not very hairy but its does have pollen-collecting hairs on it's hind legs. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2022)

File.Name: m420a_m420-004.jpg
Notes: 52017591612 I found this sleek little Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - the first one I've seen this year. I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-shaped" head. That long "neck" (actually an extended prothorax) is remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2022)

File.Name: m420b_m417-020.jpg
Notes: 52019133335 Here is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider making its web. The spider goes around and around, tacking its web to each ray as it goes. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2022)

File.Name: m420c_m417-014.jpg
Notes: 52018597521 Here are dew drops on a spider web in the grass along my trail. The drops vanish quickly when the sun hits the web, so you have to catch it right when the sun first gets to it. This web belongs to a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider. I can almost see it in its funnel-like entrance. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2022)

File.Name: m421a_m421-018.jpg
Notes: 52019641432 I believe this is a Potter Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) on leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2022)

File.Name: m421b_m421-028.jpg
Notes: 52020672766 I found this Cicada (Cicadidae) on the stem of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. It is probably genus Platypedia. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that I sometimes hear up in the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2022)

File.Name: m421c_m421-044.jpg
Notes: 52021182215 This is an odd-looking Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) that's probably the adult with fully developed wings that comes from the nymph in this photo. I believe it is genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. It's tiny, only about 1/8 inch long. Those skinny wings can't be much help for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? I found this one in a small tree of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) today. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2022)

File.Name: m422a_m422-029.jpg
Notes: 52023296815 I believe it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae -- superfamily Ichneumonoidea for sure. This is on of leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. These are parasitic wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2022)

File.Name: m422b_m422-005.jpg
Notes: 52022803418 I found this interesting Fly (Diptera) perched on on leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. At first I thought it was a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, but this one is less hairy and has more filiform antennae. Now I'm pretty sure it's another Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae) of the order Diptera). (I have a history of confusing these genera - again and again!) It's a handsome little fly with interesting mouthparts. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2022)

File.Name: m422c_m420-011.jpg
Notes: 52021715197 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2022)

File.Name: m423a_m423-029.jpg
Notes: 52025026861 This is another "true bug" on flowers of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), and it's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymph on this photo. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I also find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana) and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus). (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2022)

File.Name: m423b_m423-005.jpg
Notes: 52025249209 This is a Bumble Bee (Bombus) working a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It might be a Black-tailed Bumble Bee (Bombus melanopygus), compare this photo and JKehoe's comment. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2022)

File.Name: m423c_m422-022.jpg
Notes: 52023955097 I believe this is a Potter Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) on leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2022)

File.Name: m424a_m424-007.jpg
Notes: 52027692403 This is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly on native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The butterfly is non-native, but they do have their charms. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2022)

File.Name: m424b_m424-004.jpg
Notes: 52027623121 This isn't a caterpillar, though it's definitely caterpillar-like. It's a larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar, but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" -- with two visible differences. First, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs -- six or more pairs -- while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. Second, sawfly larvae have single eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of multiple eyespots - see this photo. This one is on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), where I've found them before -- see this photo and this photo. This larva is not small, maybe an inch long. That seems big enough to be a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) like this one, but I believe those larvae look different, compare this photo. I'm not sure what this one will become. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2022)

File.Name: m424c_m424-024.jpg
Notes: 52026538067 Here is a beetle on flowers of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The beetle is a Buprestid Beetle aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. There's a bonus creature in this photo -- a tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) in the the lower-left. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2022)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Metallic Wood-boring Beetle; Spotted Flower Buprestid; Thrips

File.Name: m425a_m425-034.jpg
Notes: 52030495049 I found this bizarre insect on a leaf of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods today. It is a Lace Bug in the family Tingidae. It's tiny, about 1/8 inch. It's my impression that the different species of Lace Bugs are specific to their hosts, and I know we have one kind on Wild Sweetpea (here) and a different kind on Yerba Santa (here). My photo today might show a third kind, though I haven't found any mention yet of these bugs on Phacelia plants. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2022)

File.Name: m425b_m425-031.jpg
Notes: 52030481024 This is an odd-looking Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) that's probably the adult with fully developed wings that comes from the nymph in this photo. I believe it is genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. It's tiny, only about 1/8 inch long. Those skinny wings can't be much help for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? I found this one in Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2022)

File.Name: m425c_m425-027.jpg
Notes: 52029174122 This isn't a caterpillar, though it's definitely caterpillar-like. It's a larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar, but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" -- with two visible differences. First, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs -- six or more pairs -- while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. Second, sawfly larvae have single eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of multiple eyespots - see this photo. This one is on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today, see this photo from yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2022)

Notes: 52032564528 This is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata, Coccinellidae) digging into a new flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods today. According to BugGuide, this kind of ladybug was introduced from Europe to control aphids and only started to spread here in 1973. If so, it's kind of frightening how quickly such an introduced species can spread across the country and into the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2022)

Notes: 52043519416 Look close . . . this is not an ant but a remarkable ant mimic! It's a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that looks and moves like a real ant, but no ant has a proboscis like that. The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae), see this photo. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. This one is on Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), but I also find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana) and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus). (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2022)

File.Name: m501c_m501-014.jpg
Notes: 52043490401 This is some spider's exuviae - the shed skin after the molt. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is. That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. There's some discussion of this at BugGuide here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2022)

Notes: 52046307338 Here is a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, but it's on the wrong plant! They usually feed on "scrophs" -- plants that were once placed in the Scrophulariaceae plant family of the figwort family, like Bush Monkeyflower or Climbing Penstemon. I've never seen one on a Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2022)

File.Name: m502c_m502-045.jpg
Notes: 52046744905 I found this small but handsome spider under a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, less than 1/8 inch of inch. The shape of its carapace and legs reminds me of a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae), but the bright colors and round shape of its abdomen seem wrong. I'm thinking it's a Cobweb Weaver aka Comb-Footed Spider in the family Theridiidae, possibly genus Theridion. It does have comb-like hairs on its legs, though they're hard to see in this photo. Whatever it is, it's an attractive spider. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2022)
CommonNames: Wild Sweetpea; Cobweb Weaver; Comb-Footed Spider

File.Name: m505a_m505-005.jpg
Notes: 52052994831 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2022)

File.Name: m505b_m505-018.jpg
Notes: 52053223124 This is the "scorpioid inflorescence" of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods today. The individual flowers are coiled like a scorpion's tail or the neck of a fiddle. They will keep producing new flowers in the curl of the stem as long as they can, with new buds developing in front (right) and fading to seed capsules behind (left). The "caterpillar" of this Phacelia refers to the long seed heads that sometimes develop when the growing season is long enough, see this photo and think of "woolly bear" caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2022)

File.Name: m517c_m517-002.jpg
Notes: 52081421524 I found these two different Ticks (Ixodidae, Arachnida) on different different plants in the woods today. I thought they were different kinds at first, but now I believe they are female and male Pacific Coast Ticks (Dermacentor occidentalis). The female ticks are larger and look different with a shield-like structure on their back, which has something to do with the fact that the males do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't - mosquitoes and deer flies are similar. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2022)

CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Mesh Web Weaver

File.Name: m518c_m514-011.jpg

The spider's orb web is on the left and its cobweb-like retreat on the right. The spider has a "sensing line" which connects its retreat to its orb web and allows it to sense the vibrations of its prey. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2022)

File.Name: m519a_m519-029.jpg
Notes: 52085713094 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the woods today. I believe this bee is family Megachilidae, maybe genus Osmia. These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees, and you can see quite a load there. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2022)

File.Name: m519b_m517-044.jpg
Notes: 52085962895 Here is a hand-sized Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I love the native thistles -- and the butterflies do too! I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2022)

File.Name: m519c_m515-014.jpg
Notes: 52085696904 This is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods. Maybe it's the species Thereva fucata, see this BugGuide info page. I've seen these handsome flies before, but not often. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2022)

File.Name: m520a_m519-018.jpg
Notes: 52086563932 This is a biting fly in our mountains drawing blood from my arm. I believe it is a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family. I got distracted taking my photo and the fly got away from me. These flies will be annoying for a week or two, then they'll disappear -- and the Deer Flies will start to appear. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2022)

File.Name: m520b_m514-051.jpg
Notes: 52087609158 Here is an Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). I believe it is genus Thevenetimyia, though I won't try to guess which species. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2022)

File.Name: m520c_m514-014.jpg
Notes: 52087601698 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a stem of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods few days ago. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2022)

File.Name: m521a_m521-033.jpg
Notes: 52089865806 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a new flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2022)

File.Name: m521b_m521-020.jpg
Notes: 52089832763 This is a well-camouflaged caterpillar on a oak branch -- or is it? This might be caterpillar-like creature is the larva of a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) maybe in the genus Abia in the order Hymenoptera. This is another one that looks like a caterpillar but isn't. These are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" -- with two visible differences. First, they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae like this always have more prolegs -- six or more pairs -- while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. I will try to find it again tomorrow and look closer. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2022)

File.Name: m521c_m521-049.jpg
Notes: 52089824373 This is looking at the remarkable face of a Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family in the woods today. You can clearly see in my photo that these large flies have the terminal segment of their palps longer than the other segments combined - those dangly bits at the end of its head. That makes them family Tipulidae. We also have smaller Crane Flies in the same group of Tipulomorpha that differ in having the terminal segment of their palps short, compare this photo of (probably) a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2022)
CommonNames: Large Crane Fly; Limoniid Crane Fly

File.Name: m522a_m522-032.jpg
Notes: 52092743135 This is a gorgeous Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I've mostly been seeing these brilliant butterflies flying up and down the canyon without ever landing, so it was a pleasure to find this one visiting this new flower today. Later in the summer, the Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies start to look cream-colored like this one, but there's no confusing them with the real thing. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2022)

File.Name: m522b_m522-007.jpg
Notes: 52092220978 This is "true bug" lounging inside a flower of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae), see this photo of the less ant-like adult. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I also find them on Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) and White Sage (Salvia apiana), but I guess these roomy flowers are another host. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2022)

File.Name: m522c_m521-027.jpg
Notes: 52092429354 Here is a nymph of a little Stilt Bug (Berytidae) on a leaf of Mexican Pink aka Indian Pink (Silene laciniata, Caryophyllaceae) in the woods yesterday. You can see just a hint of its long proboscis folded under its head. These bugs look like skinny Assassin Bugs, but I believe they only suck plants and not other insects. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2022)
CommonNames: Stilt Bug; Mexican Pink; Indian Pink

File.Name: m523a_m523-017.jpg
Notes: 52095057739 Here is a little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. These feisty butterflies seem to spend their time darting forth from a perch to defend its territory from all other passing butterflies -- and anything else that moves! This makes them hard to approach, but I got lucky with this one. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2022)

File.Name: m523b_m523-023.jpg
Notes: 52095043339 Here is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's a not the first of these butterflies that I've seen this year, but they've been scarce. This is a female, the males really are blue. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2022)

File.Name: m523c_m523-018.jpg
Notes: 52095036574 Here is a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. They are hard to photograph, with a remarkable propensity to turn away from the sun. At first they look plain brown, but when you look closer you see that they really have a rich palette of many colors like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. You can see the colors better in this photo from a few years ago. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2022)

File.Name: m524a_m524-019.jpg
Notes: 52096195322 This a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This is a female, the males really are blue. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2022)

File.Name: m524b_m522-047.jpg
Notes: 52097700115 This is a tiny spider on the petal of a Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I believe it's a Cobweb Weaver aka Comb-Footed Spider in the family Theridiidae, possibly genus Theridion or Rugathodes sexpunctatus. If you look closely at the legs, you can see little combs. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2022)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Cobweb Weaver; Comb-Footed Spider

File.Name: m524c_m519-005.jpg
Notes: 52096166762 These mating beetles are Buprestid Beetles aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetles (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably Spotted Flower Buprestids in the genus Acmaeodera. They are visiting Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2022)

File.Name: m525a_m524-004.jpg
Notes:
52099680209 This is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) taking pollen from a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) in the woods yesterday. It's too early for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2022)

I had no time for new photos today. We're heading off to Oregon for a week starting tomorrow, and there's lots to do before we leave. We have some work to do up there, but I'll try to come back with a few photos.

Notes: 52122581486 I believe this is an Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly working a new flower of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These butterflies are distinctive mostly by having nothing very distinctive about them -- plain but handsome. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2022)

They can sit quietly for a long time with their wings closed, looking quite plain, and then open their wings in a quick flash! This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. Notes: 52133250420 Here is a little Damselfly (Zygoptera, Odonata) on a oak leaf in the canyon in the woods today. I don't really know much about damselflies, and I can't identify it -- but I'm always happy to photograph one. As far as I know, the creeks are dry for a mile around -- I wonder where it come from? (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2022)

File.Name: m608b_m607-002.jpg
Notes: 52131702162 Here is tiny spider approaching its prey on a stem a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods yesterday. I believe this is a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself -- at least it matches the illustration for that genus in my book. It has the frizzy look of a cribellate spider, see this photo for an example and links for more info. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2022)

File.Name: m608c_m604-010.jpg
Notes: 52131696217 This is a large spider web in the woods. It's a couple of feet across - not huge, but the rigging lines span a good twenty feet which helps create a sense of size. This is one of those webs than can span a road that you don't want to walk into at night -- where's the spider?! I'm sure the spider is genus Araneus in the family of Orb Web Weavers (Araneidae). This would be a good Halloween photo, but here it is in June. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2022)

File.Name: m609a_m609-013.jpg
Notes: 52135189624 Here is a nymph of a little Stilt Bug (Berytidae) on a bristly seed head of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2022)

File.Name: m611a_m611-009.jpg
Notes: 52138283377 This is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly on new flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. I've grown used to having many of these butterflies around, and I'd love to find out that I'm wrong about this. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2022)

It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2022)

File.Name: m613a_m613-005.jpg
Notes: 52144905334 I found this Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on the chair that I use in the woods. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2022)

File.Name: m613b_m613-010.jpg
Notes: 52144633681 The are new flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today -- with galls. Coffeeberry has true petals in addition to sepals - those little brown flaps that surround each stamen. I guess this is why Coffeeberry and Redberry are no longer placed in the same genus. I believe the galled flowers are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), a different one from the gall in this photo but the same idea. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2022)

File.Name: m613c_m613-045.jpg
Notes: 52145112805 This is a whole plant of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's growing in an unlikely place, under the leaves of a Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family -- now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2022)
CommonNames: Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Chaparral Yucca

File.Name: m614a_m614-003.jpg
Notes: 52146310477 These are new flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today, with a galled flower in the top. That odd "crook in the neck" is typical. I believe the galled flowers are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), a different one from the gall in this photo yesterday but the same idea. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2022)

File.Name: m614b_m614-035.jpg
Notes: 52146277417 These are aphid-galled leaves of Bigberry Manzanita Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) shrub in the woods today. The tumor-like galls are caused the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. Specialized aphids live inside those pockets on the leaves. They are strange and quite beautiful when fresh like these. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2022)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid

File.Name: m614c_m612-001.jpg
Notes: 52147777900 This is a stem gall on a new plant of native Leafy Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. This is what has been called var. stenophyllus with narrow filiform leaves, compare this photo of the same gall on the the typical species plant with broad leaves. I guess the gall-inducing insects know their plants, whatever the shape of the leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2022)

File.Name: m615a_m615-039.jpg
Notes: 52150237220 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a new flower of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I was getting another shot of the remarkable Bee Fly like this when the butterfly flew into my photo. It's the first one I've seen this year. It's a female, more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, and a paler yellow color that has a hint of green. Those pointy forewings are distinctive for both male and female, and distinguish them from other Sulphur butterflies. It's an attractive butterfly, but you have to see a male with its wings open to appreciate why it is the official California State Butterfly - like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2022)
CommonNames: California Dogface; California Thistle; Bee Fly

File.Name: m615b_m615-028.jpg
Notes: 52149756853 This is a wild bee on a new flower of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe that the bee is genus Osmia in family Megachilidae. These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees, and you can see quite a load there. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2022)

File.Name: m615c_m615-015.jpg
Notes: 52148720237 This is the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider -- with the spider lurking in the entrance. The spiders sit in the opening and run out in the blink of your eye to snag any prey that bumps into its web, but they can usually tell the difference between a grass stem and real prey; they are not easy to fool. This might be an insect's idea of the entrance to hell. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2022)

File.Name: m616a_m616-044.jpg
Notes: 52152230689 This is another nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) inside a native flower of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group -- but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2022)

File.Name: m616b_m614-021.jpg
Notes: 52150970642 Here is a Blue Bottle Fly on a of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods -- or is it a Green Bottle Fly? It's hard to pin down green or blue colors with these iridescent flies. It's definitely a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae. The colors are attractive, but this is one those flies you don't want to see walking across your potato salad. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2022)
CommonNames: Blue Bottle Fly; California Coffeeberry; Green Bottle Fly; Blow Fly

File.Name: m616c_m614-008.jpg
Notes: 52151965996 This is a bristly little fly (order Diptera) on new flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods. I believe this is just another Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2022)

File.Name: m617a_m617-027.jpg
Notes: 52154676210 I found this Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden today. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2022)

File.Name: m617b_m617-002.jpg
Notes: 52154402054 Here is a dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on the little electric cart I use to get around in our canyon. I believe the fly is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa) or kin in the Villini tribe. It looks quite black in the wild, but it looks quite brown in my photos. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2022)

File.Name: m617c_m616-016.jpg
Notes: 52153127372 This is a nasty biting fly in our mountains biting brave Julie's hand yesterday! I believe it is in the family Tabanidae of Horse and Deer Flies, but it is not much larger than a house fly and its eyes are quite plain unlike many other Tabanids. I believe it is genus Apatolestes in the subfamily Pangoniinae, maybe the species Apatolestes hinei. I reckon it's OK to call it a Deer Fly even though it is not in the subfamily Chrysopsinae. Usually the Rocky Mountain Bite Flies (Symphoromyia, Rhagionidae) -- see this photo and this photo -- are the worst biters in our mountains, but lately I've been bothered more by these. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2022)
CommonNames: Deer Fly; Rocky Mountain Bite Flies

File.Name: m618a_m618-049.jpg
Notes: 52156687814 This is tiny Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2022)

File.Name: m618b_m618-046.jpg
Notes: 52156435581 Here is a tiny fly (Diptera) about to stick it's needle-like proboscis into a flower of native new flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe the fly is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae. Maybe it's in the tribe Phthiriini? (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2022)

File.Name: m618c_m618-017.jpg
Notes: 52156436103 This is a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) on leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. These tiny spiders are only about 1/8 inch long or less. Note that this one has eight spots, four on each side of its abdomen. Despite its common name, the spiders in our canyon (at least) all seem to have eight spots rather than six. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2022)

File.Name: m619a_m619-018.jpg
Notes: 52159485095 Here is a handsome little Beetle (Coleoptera) with red epaulettes on a stalk of native Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus) grass in the Poaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure it's a Red-shouldered Leaf Beetle (Saxinis saucia) in the Chrysomelidae group of beetles, at least that is the genus. It's about the size of a ladybug. It appears to be ovipositing?? (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2022)
CommonNames: Western Rye; Blue Wildrye; Red-shouldered Leaf Beetle

File.Name: m619b_m619-035.jpg
Notes: 52158953723 This is a beetle on new flower of native This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is in the genus Mecaphesa with hairy legs and abdomen, a female with slender palps. It's the kind of spider I expect to find waiting on flowers in the woods today. This is a Buprestid Beetle aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2022)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; wild bee; Prickly-Phlox; Spotted Flower Buprestid

File.Name: m619c_m616-021.jpg
Notes: 52158938106 I found this small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods a few days few days ago. Its heavily spined legs give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2022)

File.Name: m620a_m620-003.jpg
Notes: 52161858369 This a Deer Fly (family Tabanidae) on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. It's about the size of a honey bee, and just as furry. I believe it is genus Stonemyia which are not biters, maybe Stonemyia californica. Unlike most other tabanid species, none of the females of this genus are known to feed on blood. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2022)

File.Name: m620b_m620-026.jpg
Notes: 52162087210 I'm pretty sure that this a Thread-waisted Wasp in the family Sphecidae that is tangled in a spiderweb. It got away! Notice the spider, I'll make a note. I believe this is a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself. It has the frizzy look of a cribellate spider, see this photo for an example and links for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2022)

File.Name: m620c_m620-013.jpg
Notes: 52162075600 Here is another shot of a little Beetle (Coleoptera) with red epaulettes on a flower of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure it's a Red-shouldered Leaf Beetle (Saxinis saucia) in the Chrysomelidae group of beetles, at least that is the genus. It's about the size of a ladybug. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2022)

52162974397 Here is another California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a new flower of the violet form of Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata,Crassulaceae) in the woods today. File.Name: m621c_m616-007.jpg
Notes: 52162952877 This are mating Metallic Small-Headed Flies in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera -- probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. These flies seem made for Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). These flies move so fast that they are very hard to capture. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2022)

File.Name: m622a_m622-007.jpg
Notes: 52166340328 This is another little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. You can see that it's standing on two pairs of hind prolegs on the left with three pairs of true legs on the other end. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2022)

File.Name: m622b_m621-003.jpg
Notes: 52165299632 This looks like the tornado that took Dorothy to Oz! This is the web of a fairly large Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider that's being turned up by the wind. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2022)

File.Name: m622c_m619-008.jpg
Notes: 52165282262 Here is a tiny spider sitting in its web stretched of leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods a few days. File.Name: m623a_m623-010.jpg
Notes: 52168636428 I found these two different Blue butterflies in the Polyommatinae subfamily on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. On the left in a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing and on right a Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on the flowers. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2022)

File.Name: m623b_m623-003.jpg
Notes: 52167551867 This is the beautiful golden behind of an interesting fly that's hiding in a flower of native Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It's a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae -- probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. I hear these flies more often than I see them, as they fly with a high-pitched whine that is distinctive. I'll keep trying to get a better photo, but in the meantime you can search my photo for few more shots. These flies are fast and erratic, so I was happy to find this one at rest today. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2022)

Notes: 52168575188 This is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods a few days few days ago. I've only found them a few times, but that might just be because they are so well camouflaged -- at least on a neutral background like a tree branch like this. Their camouflage doesn't work so well on green leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2022)

There also a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself -- at least it matches the illustration for that genus in my book. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2022)

File.Name: m709b_m705-004.jpg
Notes: 52205418913 I found this odd-looking fly (Diptera) on a faded flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). It has remarkable eyes and interesting antennae, and it looks like it's full of eggs. The "double antennae" are actually ordinary antennae with a thickened arista -- that usually hair-like bristle that arises from the base of the antennae of most flies. I believe it is genus Lauxania in the family Lauxaniidae. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2022)

This makes them hard to approach, but I got lucky with this one. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2022)

File.Name: m714a_m714-005.jpg
Notes: 52217134124 Here are different photos of male (left) and female (right) California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies working Scabiosa flowers in the garden today. The females are greener and the males are darker with that brilliant "dog face" pattern on its upper wings. The females are pretty enough but the males take the prize as the "California State Butterfly". They are both beautiful, and it's nice to see them side by side like this. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2022)

The males do have hairy legs, but the females have even hairier legs with proper pollen baskets (scopae) designed to carry pollen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2022)

File.Name: m715c_m709-007.jpg
Notes: 52219220774 I believe this is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae, Diptera) on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods a few days ago. This is one of my favorite groups of flies -- see this photo. The larger ones like this are remarkable wasp mimics. The small ones don't seem to be mimics, but I can recognize them right off by the combination of long antennae, stout proboscis which is bent, and the curled abdomen. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2022)

File.Name: m716a_m716-023.jpg
Notes: 52219931907 Here is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly giving a peek at the dogface pattern on its upper wings. The butterfly is working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) growing in the canyon today. These fine butterflies always close their wings at rest, so it's a challenge to capture their upper wings. This is our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2022)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

This butterfly is recognizable by the row of little chevrons along the wing margin. They are more common along the river where their favorite host plant grows, Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2022)

File.Name: m717a_m717-014.jpg
Notes: 52223288618 Here is a handsome Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini aka Limenitis lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) by the creek. Usually they are in the willows nearby, but this one was chasing a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, and it settled here for a moment. Hah, the Sisters seem to taunt the Admirals! These butterflies don't seem to visit flowers. They just sit and wait -- to chase away any other butterfly that comes close to their willow tree! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2022)

File.Name: m720c_m719-038.jpg
Notes: 52230151328 Here is the another web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae). It consists of three main parts. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is random webbing that supports the bowl and might knock down flying insects into it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2022)

File.Name: m729a_m729-015.jpg
Notes: 52250509279 Here is a handsome Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini aka Limenitis lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) by the creek today. Usually they are in the willows nearby, but this one was chasing a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, and it settled here for a moment. Hah, the Admirals and Sisters seem to taunt the Admirals! These butterflies don't seem to visit flowers. They just sit and wait -- to chase away any other butterfly that comes close to their willow tree. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2022)

File.Name: m729b_m725-015.jpg
Notes: 52249238792 This is Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods few days ago. I believe it is genus Villa in the tribe Villini. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2022)

File.Name: m729c_m725-004.jpg
Notes: 52250703795 I found this Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a on leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods few days ago. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2022)

File.Name: m730a_m730-024.jpg
Notes: 52252345998 Here is another female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2022)

File.Name: m730b_m730-012.jpg
Notes: 52251324652 I found this "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it's another Stink Bug in the family Pentatomoidea with 5-segmented antennae. Look, it's taking off! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2022)

File.Name: m730c_m730-021.jpg
Notes: 52252571384 This is another Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) working flowers of of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Yep, they have remarkable goggle-like eyes, but what gives with that amazing "third eye" between its two real eyes -- the mark of the Illuminati?? These flowers are mostly finished, but they keep putting out small axillary flowers that still draws a crowd. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2022)

File.Name: m731a_m731-022.jpg
Notes: 52253683342 I believe this is an Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly working a new flower of the White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. These butterflies are distinctive mostly by having nothing very distinctive about them -- plain but handsome, see this photo. That dark streak on the forewings is the butterfly's stigma -- a glandular patch that releases pheromones during courtship that only males have. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2022)

CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

52264999257 This is a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on a new composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia, Asteraceae) that's just starting to open in the woods today. I believe the fly is genus Villa in the tribe Villini. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2022)

File.Name: m813a_m813-036.jpg
Notes: 52283796683 Here is a little wild bee on a new flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure this is a Masked Bee aka Yellow-faced Bee in the genus Hylaeus. These tiny bees lack pollen-collecting hairs but instead carry pollen and nectar in their gut and regurgitate it when they get back to their nests. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2022)
CommonNames: California Aster; Masked Bee; Yellow-faced Bee

File.Name: m815b_m815-017.jpg

I believe the bugs are species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The spines and striped legs are remarkable. This little predator is quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2022)

(San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2022)

File.Name: m816b_m816-035.jpg
Notes: 52291132154 Here are a mating pair of Stink Bugs in the family Pentatomidae with 5-segmented antennae on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. I find this a confusing group, but this one might be the Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens), though there are other possibilities. If it is that species, they don't always have that red line across the thorax - in fact, sometimes they look completely brown. Like I said, it's a confusing group -- but they are handsome bugs. It's almost Wednesday, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2022)
CommonNames: Bird's Beak; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug

File.Name: m819a_m819-044.jpg
Notes: 52297672209 I was getting a photograph of this frog when a wasp landed on it! The frog is a getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. This is probably a Baja California Chorus Frog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca), but I can't really tell from this photo. We seem to be right on the line between this species and the Pacific Chorus Frog (P. sierra), so who knows. (There used to be just the Pacific Treefrog (Pseudacris regilla or Hyla regilla), but the species was split up -- those darn herpetologists!) The wasp is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2022)
CommonNames: Baja California Chorus Frog;  Pacific Chorus Frog; Western Paper Wasp

File.Name: m819b_m818-005.jpg
Notes: 52297394943 Here is an attractive little fly (Diptera) on a dead leaf of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it might be species Minettia flaveola in the family Lauxaniidae -- compare this photo at BugGuide, especially the hairs on its head. Another possibility is the genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae, though the larvae of those flies are said to develop in fungi, and it's the wrong time of year for that. I find these flies every year, and I still get confused by them every time. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2022)
